segments.s221-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan' and بدان (written here as ب + دان) is a demonstrative/locative 'at that' or 'toward that' referring back to what was described; پر و پیکان تیر lists the objects 'feather and (arrow) shaft/arrow', joined by و 'and'. Word order is standard for an initial temporal clause: 'When [subject] [to/at-that] [objects] ...'.
segments.s221-g106: نگه کرد is a compound verb meaning 'fixed his gaze / looked' (نگه 'gaze' + کرد 'did/made'), with کرد as the past simple. برنا modifies the possessor of دل: 'his youth(ful) [heart]' and دلش گشت پیر is a small clause 'his heart became old' where گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/turn' and پیر 'old' is the predicate adjective; ش is the pronominal enclitic 'his'.
segments.s221-g107: به پیران 'to the elders' is an indirect-object prepositional phrase (به + plural پیران). چنین گفت literally 'said thus' — گفت here is the verb 'said' (though the provided senses list nouns; grammatically this is past tense). کین مرد کیست? is a quoted interrogative clause 'who is this man?' (کیست = who/is), where کین functions as a demonstrative 'this' in older/poetic usage; the clause is the content of what was said.
segments.s221-g108: ز گردان ایران ورا نام چیست؟ is a free-standing interrogative: ز 'from/of' marks source or sphere (poetic for از), گردان ایران 'the troop(s) of Iran', ورا 'to him' (meaning 'of him' or 'regarding him') and نام چیست 'what is the name? / what is the name (of him)?' The copula چیست places the question-word نام چی (what name) before it — typical Persian question order.
segments.s221-g109: تو at the start is the vocative/2nd-person subject 'you'. گفتی is the past form 'you said' (here followed by the complement clause introduced by که 'that'). لختی is an adverbial 'for a short while' or 'briefly', and فرومایه‌اند is a present/past-summary statement 'they are base/despicable' with the plural copula اند (here fused as‌اند). The clause as a whole reports what 'you said'.
segments.s221-g110: ز گردنکشان here means 'from/among the گردنکشان' (poetic use of ز = از). گردنکشان is a plural noun referring to rebellious/proud/neck-breaking ones; کمترین is a superlative 'the least' or 'smallest', and پایه‌اند ('they are foundations') uses the plural copula‌اند. Together the phrase asserts that, among those proud ones, 'they' rank as the least foundation or the most insignificant element.
segments.s221-g111: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. نیزه با تیر ایشان یکیست is a verbless/copular statement where یکیست 'is one' predicateally unites نیزه 'spear' and تیر 'arrow' with با 'with' indicating the pairing; ایشان is the possessive 'their'. The sense is that spear and arrow function together or are indistinguishable/one in effect.
segments.s221-g112: دل شیر در جنگشان اندکیست is a predicative clause: دل شیر 'lion-heart' (courage) with دل as the noun and شیر qualifying it. در جنگشان 'in their fighting/battle' gives the domain, and اندکیست 'there is little' (it is small) states the lack — idiomatically 'they have little lion's heart in battle' (i.e., they lack courage).
segments.s221-g265: بزرگان is the subject ('the great men, nobles'); ز هر جای is a poetic prepositional phrase ('from every place/quarter') placed before the verb; برخاستند is a past‑tense 3rd‑person plural verb ('they rose/rose up'). The order Subject + PP + Verb is normal in Persian narrative, and ز is the archaic poetic form of از.
segments.s221-g266: به خاقان چین is an indirect object phrase ('to/for the Khagan of China'); خواهش is the noun 'request/petition' and آراستند is an archaic/poetic past verb here meaning 'they arranged/offered/expressed' the petition. The verb is transitive and takes the petition as its object directed to the khagan.
segments.s221-g267: که introduces a content subordinate clause ('that...'); بر لشکر is the prepositional phrase 'over the army' (بر = over/against); امروز is an adverb ('today'); فرمان تراست is an equational predicate: فرمان (the command) + تراست (is yours). تراست is an older/poetic form meaning 'it is for you' (تَ + است).
segments.s221-g268: همه کشور چین و توران is the nominal subject ('the whole countries/realm of China and Turan') and تراست repeats the copular construction 'is yours.' همه here modifies کشور and carries the sense 'whole/entire.' Word order is Subject + Predicate with the possessive sense encoded in تراست.
segments.s221-g269: یک امروز here functions as a temporal phrase ('now/one day today'); بنگر is an imperative ('look, behold'); بدین is the fused form of به + این meaning 'to/at this' (here 'at this battlefield'). The imperative beneger commands attention to the following noun رزمگاه ('battlefield').
segments.s221-g270: که introduces the explanatory clause ('for/that'); شمشیر is personified as the subject ('sword(s)'); بارد here is a poetic present verb from باریدن ('to fall/rain') meaning 'rains/falls' — this sense is not among the provided options; ز = 'from'; ابر سیاه = 'black cloud.' The whole clause literally says 'that sword(s) rain from the black cloud.'
segments.s221-g271: وزین روی is an adverbial phrase of manner ('with a weighty/grave face'); it modifies the following clause. رستم is the subject; به ایرانیان marks the indirect object/recipient ('to the Iranians'). The adjective وزین gives a formal, dignified tone to Rostam's address.
segments.s221-g272: چنین گفت is a formulaic phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — the provided senses for گفت do not include the verb meaning, so its sense is marked below as not matched). کاکنون is an emphatic temporal adverb 'now/at this very moment'; سرآمد زمان literally 'the end/arrival of time' (i.e. 'the time has come' or 'the moment is upon us'). Together this reports Rostam's speech: 'Thus he said: now the time has come.'
segments.s221-g153: The clause begins with the prepositional phrase 'به بالای او بر زمین' (literally 'to/at the top of him on the ground'), fronted for poetic emphasis; 'به بالای' places something 'above' someone, followed by the pronoun 'او' (he). 'بر زمین' literally 'on the earth/ground' narrows the domain. The core predication is the existential negation 'مرد نیست' — 'there is no man' or 'no (worthy) man exists' — where the noun 'مرد' is negated by the copula/existential 'نیست'.
segments.s221-g154: 'بدین لشکر' functions as 'in/among this army' (poetic compound where بـ- marks direction/relation to 'دین'/'this'), placing the domain; 'او را' uses the object/topic marker 'را' after the pronoun to mark the person as the one compared. 'هم‌آورد' is a substantive meaning 'equal' or 'rival', and 'نیست' negates it — together 'او را هم‌آورد نیست' = 'there is no equal to him'. The structure is thus: domain → target-marked pronoun → predicate 'no equal exists'.
segments.s221-g155: 'کمانش' is the noun 'کمان' (bow) with the possessive suffix '-ش' = 'his bow'. 'تو دیدی' is second-person singular past 'you saw' (subject 'تو' before the verb for emphasis). The conjunction 'و' links the next noun phrase 'تیر ایدرست' — here 'تیر' (arrow) followed by an adjective/epithet 'ایدرست' (poetic/archaic expression meaning 'true/straight' or emphatic), so the phrase means roughly 'and his true/straight arrow.'
segments.s221-g156: This clause gives a comparison by means of power: 'به زور او' uses 'به' with an instrumental/agentive sense ('by/with his strength') and the pronoun 'او' ('his'). The preposition 'ز' here is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'than/from' in comparisons; 'پیل ژیان' = 'elephants that are alive/living elephants' (ژیان qualifying پیل). The verb 'برترست' is an archaic/poetic third-person singular meaning 'is superior/surpasses', so the clause reads 'by his strength he surpasses living elephants.'
segments.s221-g157: 'همانا' is a vocative/expletive particle meaning 'verily' or 'indeed' used to introduce or emphasize the statement. 'که' here is the relative/linking particle 'that/who', and 'آن سگزی جنگجوی' is the noun phrase 'that dog-like/base warrior' (سگزی is a derogatory adjective placed before the noun 'جنگجوی'). Word order (adjective before noun) and the fronted demonstrative 'آن' are typical poetic choices to heighten insult and emphasis.
segments.s221-g158: This is a relative clause modifying the warrior: 'که' again links the clause; 'چندین' means 'several' or 'many a'; 'همی' is an archaic auxiliary/particle often used in older Persian to add emphasis or durative flavor (here emphatic/intensifying before the verb); 'برشمردی' is second-person singular past 'you counted/enumerated'; 'ازوی' = 'of/from him' (the source/target of the counting). The clause thus states 'whom you counted many of' with SOV order common in Persian poetry.
segments.s221-g159: 'پیاده' functions adverbially here as 'on foot' (or as a noun 'infantry') placed before the place phrase. 'بدین رزمگاه' is a poetic/demonstrative prepositional phrase 'to/at this battlefield' ('بدین' = 'to/at this'); 'آمدست' is an archaic/perfect-form third-person singular meaning 'has come' or 'came.' The order (manner → place → verb) is characteristic of Persian poetic compression.
segments.s221-g160: 'به یاری' means 'to the aid/support of' (preposition + noun), followed by the name 'ایران' as the beneficiary. 'سپاه آمدست' places the subject 'سپاه' (army) before the verb 'آمدست' (archaic 'came/has come'), giving 'an army came to Iran's aid.' The use of 'آمدست' is formal/poetic past perfect in Ferdowsi's language.
segments.s221-g273: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). کُشته شد is a passive past construction (کشته + شد), here meaning 'were killed' (شد as the auxiliary 'be' in passive). زین سپاه is a genitive/identifying phrase meaning 'this host/army' (zīn is an older demonstrative/identifier in context), and اندکی follows to quantify the victims: 'a few (were) killed.' Word order is If + patient killed + auxiliary + source + quantifier.
segments.s221-g274: نشد is the negative past of شدن used to mean 'did not happen / there was not'. The following phrase بیش و کم از دو سیصد یکی is the quantity being negated: 'more or less than one out of two hundred.' بیش و کم functions as a fixed pair 'more and less' = 'more or less,' از marks the partitive 'out of,' and یکی is the numeral 'one.'
segments.s221-g275: چنین یکسره are adverbial intensifiers 'so / thus, completely.' دل مدارید is a present/imperative‑style verb phrase addressed to the second person plural (مدارید = 'do/may you spare' or 'be lenient with your hearts'); here the poet admonishes: 'do not be so merciful/soft‑hearted.' تنگ (literally 'narrow') modifies مدارید/del to mean 'close‑hearted' or 'too sparing; not generous of heart.'
segments.s221-g276: نخواهم is first‑person negative 'I do not want / I will not.' تن زنده is an object-like nominal phrase 'a living body' (تن = body, زنده = alive). بی‌نام و ننگ are two adjectives joined by و describing the desired life: 'nameless and shameful' (i.e. without fame and honor). The clause states the speaker's refusal: 'I will not want to remain a living body without name and honor.'
segments.s221-g277: همه modifies لشکر ترک: 'the whole army of Turks.' از marks source or association: 'from Ashkabus' (اشکبوس is a proper name here). The phrase gives who moved and from whom/where they were associated.
segments.s221-g278: برفتند is simple past, third‑person plural 'they departed / went away.' رخساره چون سندروس is a simile predicated on that subject: رخساره 'the faces/complexions' + چون 'like' + سندروس (a fragrant/colored wood) yields 'their faces were like sandalwood' — a poetic image for complexion.
segments.s221-g279: کنون 'now' and یک‌سره 'wholly/completely' are fronted adverbials. دل پر از کین is a nominal phrase 'hearts full of vengeance' (دل + پر 'full' + از کین 'of hatred/vengeance'). کنید is second‑person plural imperative/polite command ('make/do'), so the clause commands: 'Now make your hearts wholly full of vengeance.'
segments.s221-g280: بروهای جنگی is a plural nominal compound 'those who go to war / the war‑goers (i.e. warriors or fighters).' The parallel predicate پر از چین کنید uses the same imperative کنید and means 'make (them) full of چین' — چین here = 'wrinkles, furrows' (figuratively, stern lines of anger). The verb is elliptically linked to the previous clause; both are commands addressed to the same addressees.
segments.s221-g249: چنین گفت خاقان is a short reported-speech header: Literally “thus said the Khaghan.” Classical Persian often places the adverb πριν the verb (چنین گفت) before the speaker (خاقان). که here begins the reported content ('that...').
segments.s221-g250: نباید is an impersonal negative modal meaning 'it should not / must not be'; it governs the subordinate clause introduced by که (here functioning as 'because' or 'that'). چون + دی = 'as/yet like yesterday' and بود is the past copula 'was' (used descriptively here). با درنگ = 'with delay' (با = 'with', درنگ = 'delay'). Together: 'نباید که چون دی بود با درنگ' = 'it must not be, for it would be like yesterday, with delay.'
segments.s221-g251: گمان برد is a classical collocation meaning 'he supposed' or 'he formed the conjecture' (گمان + بردن). باید here adds necessity/assessment ('must / it is necessary'), and که introduces the content clause 'that the elders were not (present)'. نبود is the past negative of بودن, 'were not.'
segments.s221-g252: نه ... نشاید is a negation: نه 'not' + نشاید 'it is not fitting/appropriate.' بی او = 'without him' (preposition-like بی + pronoun او). نبرد آزمود literally 'to try/battle' — آزمود (past participle of آزمودن) here functions like an infinitival idea 'to attempt/try a battle.' The clause means 'nor is it right to attempt battle without him.'
segments.s221-g253: همه همگنان رزمساز is a noun phrase 'all (of) the comrades/peers who are warriors.' The verb آمدیم is first-person plural past 'we came'; Persian often places the verb after the full noun phrase. The phrase asserts that all the comrades/warriors have arrived.
segments.s221-g254: به یاری = 'to/for help' expressing purpose ('to help'). ز راه دراز = a poetic ablative 'from a long road' (ز = 'from', راه = 'road', دراز = 'long'). The repeated آمدیم (we came) emphasizes their arrival and links purpose and origin: 'we came to help, coming from a long road.'
segments.s221-g255: گر is the conditional 'if.' امرو‌ز + clause 'چون دی درنگ آوریم' = 'if today we delay as (we did) yesterday.' درنگ 'delay' is the noun and آوریم (subjunctive/present plural) 'we bring / we cause' functions as the verb of the protasis here ('if we cause/delay...'). Word order places the conditional marker and temporal adverb before the verb at the clause end.
segments.s221-g256: همه نام را = 'all (our) names/reputations' with را marking the definite object. زیر ننگ = 'under disgrace/shame' (زیر = 'under', ننگ = 'shame'). آوریم repeats the verb 'we will bring' (here the apodosis/result of the conditional): 'we shall bring all our names under shame.'
segments.s221-g129: This whole clause is 'to the elders thus said Homan Gard' with the indirect-object preposition be + recipient first (به پیران = 'to the elders'), then the adverb چنین ('thus'), then the verb گفت ('said'). هومان is the speaker's name; گرد here is a name-element or epithet attached to Homan (part of his full name/title). Word order is topical: indirect object first, then manner, then speech verb and speaker.
segments.s221-g130: که introduces a reported clause ('that ...'). دشمن ندارد is a present-tense predicate (دارد with negative ندارد) meaning 'does not have an enemy' (third-person singular). خردمند خرد is a compact phrase: خردمند is 'the wise/man of reason' and خرد is 'wisdom'; here the two words stand together in apposition/juxtaposition — the clause asserts something about the wise (lit. 'the wise [one], wisdom').
segments.s221-g131: بزرگان ایران is a genitival noun phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (title/people of Iran). گشاده‌دلند is an adjectival predicate in the present plural ('are broad‑hearted/generous'), built from گشاده‌دل plus the 3rd‑plural ending -ند.
segments.s221-g132: تو گویی is an idiomatic formula meaning 'you would say' or 'it seems as if' (literally 'you say'), followed by که introducing the subordinate clause. آهن is the direct object 'iron' (poetic for hard metal/weapon), همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle, and بگسلند is the classical 3rd‑plural form of گسستن with the verbal prefix بـ (they would/shall split/break). The sequence expresses a hypothetical impression: 'you would say that they would break iron.'
segments.s221-g133: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. تا + verb here introduces the temporal/conditional sense 'when/as soon as' (کنون تا بیامد = 'now, when … comes'). بیامد is a classical form meaning 'comes / may come' referring to سپاه ('the army') which follows as the subject; از ایران specifies origin 'from Iran'.
segments.s221-g134: همی is the archaic particle giving a continuous/emphatic shade. برخروشند is 3rd‑plural present/subjunctive of برخاستن/خروشیدن 'to rise up; to burst forth'. زان is a contracted classical form meaning 'from that' (از + آن) and رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield'; together: 'they will burst forth from that battlefield.'
segments.s221-g135: بدو ('to him') is the prepositional phrase indicating the addressee; گفت پیران is 'the elders said' with subject پیران following the verb (common in epic diction). که introduces the content clause. هر چند is an idiomatic concessive/quantifying phrase meaning 'however many' or 'although many', and یار here means 'ally/companion' (i.e., warriors who might come).
segments.s221-g136: بیاید is an optative/ subjunctive classical form 'may come' or 'come' used here to express a hypothetical ('however many ... may come'). بر with طوس indicates direction/target 'against/upon Tus' (طوس is the addressee or leader Tus), از ایران marks origin 'from Iran', and سوار is a noun 'horseman/horsemen' — together: 'may come, mounted men from Iran against Tus.'
segments.s221-g9: بر + demonstrative + adjective + noun precedes the verb: بر آن نامور تیرباران = “upon that renowned (one), a shower of arrows”. تیرباران is a compound noun 'shower of arrows'. گرفت is a 3rd-person past tense used here intransitively — common in poetry to mean ‘fell / set upon / began (to happen)’.
segments.s221-g10: کمانش = کمان + possessive suffix -ش (‘his/its bow’). کمین سواران is a noun phrase 'the ambush of the horsemen' (ezāfe omitted in poetry), placed before the verb. گرفت again is past tense ‘seized/took’ used here as ‘took aim at / fixed on’.
segments.s221-g11: جهان‌جوی is an epithet or agent noun 'world‑seeker' used as the subject. در زیر پولاد is a locative phrase 'in/underneath steel' (i.e. beneath blades/armor). بود is the past copula 'was'. Word order is subject + locative + copula in a compact poetic line.
segments.s221-g12: به here introduces the circumstance or means ('by/with' or 'in the event of'); خفتانش is a nominalized/poetic form with the possessive suffix -ش (roughly 'his falling' or 'his being felled'). بر تیر = 'upon an arrow'. چون باد بود is a simile 'he was like the wind', with بود the past copula. The whole clause describes the manner of his fall.
segments.s221-g13: نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past (there was not / did not exist). کارگر تیر = 'an effective arrow' (کارگر here = effective). بر گبر اوی = 'against that infidel of his' (بر = against/onto; اوی = emphatic/old-form pronoun 'him'). The clause means there was no arrow that took effect against that man.
segments.s221-g14: از آن introduces cause or comparison ('from/ because of that'). تیزتر شد = comparative + past 'became keener/sharper'. دل جنگجوی = 'the warrior's heart' (noun + possessor), placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s221-g15: به گرز گران دست برد is a verbal phrase where دست برد (put/laid hand to, took up) combines with the object 'the heavy mace' (گرز گران). به here introduces the object/goal of the action. اشکبوس is the agent (proper name). In context برد is past tense of بردن used in the compound 'دست برد' meaning 'took up/laid hands on'.
segments.s221-g16: Two short nominal-predicative clauses: زمین آهنین شد = 'the earth became iron' (شد = became). سپهر آبنوس repeats the noun-adjective pair with the verb omitted by ellipsis (i.e. 'the sky (became) ebony'). This is a poetic way to describe the battlefield turning hard/dark.
segments.s221-g201: ابا here is an archaic verb meaning “to shrink/be reluctant”; the clause opens with this verb phrase and functions like a conditional (“if you shrink/are averse”). این شگفتی is a noun phrase “this marvel/astonishment” (demonstrative + noun). به‌روز نبرد places the action in daylight—به‌روز (“of the day”) modifies نبرد (“battle/fighting”); word order (modifier after demonstrative, then time phrase + noun) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s221-g202: This clause is conditional/elliptical. سزدگر functions as a predicative adjective/participle meaning “fitting/appropriate” (literally “it would be fitting”); نداری تو literally ‘you have not/you lack’ (here understood as ‘if you cannot/if you do not have/retain him’). او را is the definite object (او with the object marker را), and به مرد means “to a man/into the care of a man.” The verb that would explicitly mean “give/entrust” is omitted but implied, so the whole reads “if you cannot keep him, it is fitting to give him to a man.”
segments.s221-g203: چو is the conjunction “when.” بشنید is the past 3rd-person verb “he heard.” کاموس is a proper name (here the person who hears); it stands before the epithet بسیارهوش (“very clever”), which in classical Persian can follow the name as an epithet.
segments.s221-g204: This clause has the verb سپرد (“entrusted/handed over”) followed by time phrase آن زمان (“at that time”) and then the objects چشم و گوش (“eyes and ears”). The indirect object is marked by به پیران (“to the elders”): به + noun indicates the recipient. Word order (verb + time + objects) is natural in older Persian.
segments.s221-g205: همانا is an emphatic particle “truly/indeed.” خوش آمدش is a fixed verbal phrase meaning “it pleased him” (خوش ‘pleasant’ + آمد‌ش ‘came to him’ where -ش is the enclitic for ‘to him/her’). گفتار اوی is a genitive phrase “his speech/words” (گفتار + اوی, the older possessive form equivalent to ‘his’). The clause means “indeed his words pleased him.”
segments.s221-g206: برافروخت is past tense ‘it kindled/set ablaze.’ زان کار is a prepositional phrase ‘from/for that deed/affair’ (زان = ‘from that’, کار = ‘deed/affair’). بازار اوی literally ‘his market’ (اوی = his) is the thing kindled—here a metaphorical expression meaning his public standing or business was stirred. Word order: verb + prepositional phrase + object (possessive noun).
segments.s221-g207: به پیران چنین گفت is “he said thus to the elders” (به + indirect object پیران = ‘to the elders’; چنین = ‘thus’, and گفت here is the past verb ‘said’—note: in the supplied senses گفت is listed as ‘conversation/dialogue’, but in the line it functions as the verb ‘said’). کای پهلوان is a vocative: کای = “O” (vocative particle, common in epic diction) followed by پهلوان “champion/hero.”
segments.s221-g208: This is a direct address and imperative: تو = “you,” بیدار دل باش = imperative ‘be wakeful/alert of heart’ (بیدار modifies دل; باش is the imperative ‘be’), و روشن‌روان = ‘and bright‑souled’ (روشن‌روان is a compound epithet). The syntax is straightforward: vocative + two predicative imperatives joined by و.
segments.s221-g257: This is an introductory subordinate phrase: 'و' (and) joins this clause to the previous lines, 'دیگر' here means 'furthermore/another (day)', and 'که' is the subordinating conjunction 'that/when'. 'فردا' (tomorrow) is the temporal adverb, and the preposition 'ز' precedes the proper name 'افراسیاب' (Afrasiab) — a poetic order where the preposition comes directly before the name.
segments.s221-g258: 'سپاس' here means 'tribute/thanks' as the thing to be sought. 'اندر' is the poetic preposition 'in/within', linking to the following noun 'آرام' (rest/quiet). 'جوییم' is 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative (we seek / let us seek / we should seek) and takes the preceding nouns as its object; final 'خواب' (sleep) is coordinated by 'و' with 'آرام'.
segments.s221-g259: 'یکی رزم' (a single/one battle) places the numeral and noun up front for emphasis. 'باید' is the impersonal modal 'must/it is necessary', and 'همه هم‌گروه' is the subject phrase ('all, united as one group') which is completed by the infinitive in the next segment.
segments.s221-g260: The verbal infinitive 'شدن' ('to become' / 'to be') completes the obligation expressed by 'باید' — here meaning 'must become / must join'. 'پیش لشکر' is a prepositional phrase 'in front of / before the army/host'. 'به کردار کوه' is a simile: 'به' introduces the manner ('in the fashion/like') and 'کردار کوه' literally 'in behavior/stance of a mountain' = 'standing like a mountain'.
segments.s221-g261: This is the object phrase to the imperative that follows: 'ز من' (literally 'from/of me' in poetic usage here = 'for me' / 'on my behalf'), followed by the two coordinated nouns 'هدیه و برده زابلی' = 'a gift and a Zābeli slave' (برده here = 'slave, captive').
segments.s221-g262: 'بیابید' is an imperative/subjunctive (2nd-person plural) form meaning 'find/obtain'. 'با' means 'with/by means of', and the archaic noun 'شاره' denotes a ruler/sovereign here; 'کابلی' is the adjectival form 'of Kabul' modifying 'شاره'. The whole phrase tells attendants to procure the items via the Kabul ruler.
segments.s221-g263: This clause uses the partitive/relational 'ز' ('of/from') with a numeral phrase 'ده کشور' ('ten countries') followed by the locative 'ایدر' ('here'). 'سرافراز هست' is the copular predicate 'is exalted / stands lofty' — classical Persian often separates the genitive phrase and the predicate as seen here.
segments.s221-g264: The repeated preposition 'به' introduces two gerunds/nouns 'خواب' (sleep) and 'خوردن' (eating) joined by 'و'. 'نباید' is the impersonal prohibition 'one must not / it is not proper to', and 'نشست' here is the verb 'to sit' (i.e. to sit idly/settle). The sense: one should not sit idly in sleep and eating (i.e. be idle).
segments.s221-g49: This is a prepositional phrase fronted for emphasis: به شهر تو = “into/to your city” (به used spatially). It is followed by a coordinated list of nouns (شیر و نهنگ و پلنگ), so the whole segment names the three figures that are the topic of the clause.
segments.s221-g50: سوار is a noun ‘rider/horseman’ and appears with the locative/old-form preposition اندر; آیند is a literary third-person plural present/subjunctive meaning ‘they come/enter’. هر سه (‘all three’) quantifies them. به جنگ is a directional/purpose phrase ‘to (the) battle’. The verb appears after the (fronted) subject phrase, a common poetic order.
segments.s221-g51: هم‌اکنون is an emphatic temporal adverb ‘right now’. ترا‌ای is the object pronoun ترا with the vocative particle ای attached (archaic/poetic vocative: “O you”). نبرده is a negative perfect/participle form (lit. ‘not taken/carried’) used attributively to characterize the addressee (‘not having been carried/borne’ → ‘not previously mounted’), followed by سوار ‘rider’ — together giving an epithet: “you, O not-yet-mounted rider”.
segments.s221-g52: پیاده here specifies manner/force type ‘on foot’ (infantry). بیاموزمت is a literary 1st-person verb meaning ‘I will teach you’ (the verb carries the object as a clitic). کارزار is the direct object meaning ‘battle/warfare’ or ‘the art of battle’; word order places the manner (پیاده) before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s221-g53: This clause has a topical fronted adverb پیاده ‘on foot’. مرا (here split in the tokenization) is the definite direct object ‘me’, marked by را. زان ‘from there’ gives the source, فرستاد is the past verb ‘sent’, and طوس (agent) comes at the end — a normal Persian order where the subject/agent may follow the verb for emphasis.
segments.s221-g54: که ... تا introduces a purposive/subjunctive clause: که تا = ‘so that / in order that’. بستانم is a 1st-person singular subjunctive form ‘(that) I may take/seize’ (–م marking the speaker). اسپ is the object ‘horse/steed’, and از marks the source ‘from Ashkabus’ (a proper name).
segments.s221-g55: کشانی is a verbal form from کشاند/کشیدن used conditionally/hypothetically here (‘if you bring/lead’ or ‘should you bring’); it governs پیاده ‘infantry/on foot’. شود is a literary 3rd-person singular subjunctive/auxiliary ‘become/will be’, and همچو من ‘like me’ is a simile modifying the manner/standard of bringing.
segments.s221-g56: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’; دو روی خندان literally ‘two smiling faces’ (i.e. both sides smiling) is the cause/description, شوند is the 3rd-person plural subjunctive ‘they will become/are made’, and انجمن ‘assembly/gathering’ is the affected group. Poetic inversion places verbal and nominal elements in an order differing from modern prose.
segments.s221-g161: بدو is the prepositional clitic meaning 'to him' (to + pronoun); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here placed before the subject because of poetic inversion); پیران is the speaker's name (or elder(s) in other contexts) and functions as the subject; که introduces a reported clause; او دیگرست is the subordinate clause 'he is different/other', with دیگرست an archaic poetic copula ('دیگر' + 'است').
segments.s221-g162: سواری is the noun 'rider/horseman' (the head of this nominal phrase); سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'lofty / triumphant / proud' modifying سواری; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; کنداورست is another epithet or compound adjective attached to the rider (a poetic/rare word — see sense note). Together they form a noun phrase describing the man.
segments.s221-g163: بترسید is a past-tense verb 'feared' (third-person narrative); پس here means 'then' or 'thereupon' and introduces the sequence; مرد بیدار دل is a noun phrase 'the man of waking/alert heart' where بیدار describes the man and دل is a genitival apposition ('man with an awakened heart'). The verb precedes the subject in this poetic order.
segments.s221-g164: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; بسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate 'bound' used with the auxiliary بود ('was') to form the pluperfect/previous state; بود is the past auxiliary 'was'; اندرَان (poetic contraction of اندرِ آن) means 'in that/therein' and links to کار دل 'the matter/affair of the heart' (کار = affair, دل = the heart). Word order places the participle before the auxiliary as in classical Persian.
segments.s221-g165: ز (poetic from/از) marks the source 'from Piran' or 'of Piran'; بپرسید here is a narrative past 'he asked / inquired' (third person); کان is an archaic demonstrative/relative introducing the following epithet/clause; شیر مرد is a compound epithet 'lion-man' meaning a lion‑hearted/valiant man (شیر = lion, مرد = man/hero).
segments.s221-g166: چگونه is the interrogative 'how/ in what manner'; خرامد is the third-person form of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance/proudly walk' (classical/poetic present/past narrative sense); به + دشت together mean 'into/to the plain' (دشت = plain); نبرد is the noun 'battle' and here names the battlefield — 'into the plain of battle'. The clause asks about the manner of his entry into battle.
segments.s221-g167: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (از); بازو here denotes physical arm and by extension 'strength'; و links بازو to برزش (his prowess/valor); برزش is a possessive-form noun 'his worth/greatness' (برز = greatness, -ش = his/its in classical usage); چه داری is 'what do you have' (چه = what, داری = you have / you possess); نشان is the direct object 'sign / token'. The phrase asks for a token/evidence of his strength and greatness.
segments.s221-g168: چه is 'what'; گوید is the classical/present-tense or narrative-form of 'say' (used here in a rhetorical/future-oriented sense 'what will he say' or 'what could he say'); به + آورد is a verbal construction where آورد (from آوردن) 'brings/produces' can mean 'utter/offer' — literally 'what will he bring/say'; با is the preposition 'with' or 'against' and سرکشان (poetic plural of سرکش) means 'the proud/rebellious', so the clause asks rhetorically what he would say or produce when facing the proud.
segments.s221-g41: Word order is subject–object–predicate with some fronting for emphasis: مرا (me) + مادرم (my mother, subject with possessive -م) + نام مرگ تو (the name “your Death” where تو follows مرگ as a postposed possessive) + کرد (past tense, 3rd sg. ‘made/gave’). Here کرد functions as the light verb ‘to give/make’ used with نام to mean ‘call/name’.
segments.s221-g42: زمانه (fate/the age) is the subject, followed by مرا + را (the direct object, ‘me’ with object marker), then the predicate nominal پتک ترگ تو (a compound “mallet of rags” with تو as postposed possessive), and کرد (past ‘made’). The construction means ‘fate made me into your rag‑mallet’; ترگ modifies پتک.
segments.s221-g43: کشانی is a verbal/agentive form related to کشاندن (to drag/bring) and functions here as ‘the one who dragged’ or ‘he who dragged’, بدو = ‘to him’, گفت = ‘said’ (past). بی‌بارگی is used as the content of the speech or an adverbial phrase meaning ‘without burden / lightly / barren’ depending on context; here it introduces the manner or an insulting descriptor before the command.
segments.s221-g44: به کشتن is a prepositional/verbal noun phrase ‘to killing / to be killed’, دهی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive ‘you give / you deliver’, سر is the direct object ‘(his) head’, and به یک‌بارگی is an adverbial phrase ‘in one stroke / all at once’. Together they form the command/statement ‘give the head to killing at once’ (i.e. kill him in one blow).
segments.s221-g45: تهمتن is the speaker’s name/epithet (subject), چنین ‘thus’ modifies the manner of the response, داد here is the past verb ‘gave’ (i.e. ‘gave an answer’), پاسخ is the noun ‘answer/reply’, and بدوی is an adverbial/qualifying word describing the reply as crude or immediate (archaic/poetic usage).
segments.s221-g46: که introduces the quoted speech (like ‘(saying) that’), ای (here attached within که‌ای) is the vocative particle addressing someone, بیهده is an adjectival insult ‘senseless/futile’, مرد is the vocative ‘man’ (addressed person), and پرخاشجوی describes him as ‘quarrelsome/aggressive’. Together they form the direct vocative: ‘O senseless, quarrelsome man!’
segments.s221-g47: پیاده here means ‘on foot’ (or ‘infantry’) and heads the clause; ندیدی is the 2nd‑person past negative ‘did you not see?’. که introduces the subordinate clause ‘that/because’, جنگ is ‘fight/battle’, and آورد is the past verb ‘brought/caused’. The whole is a rhetorical question: ‘Didn’t you see (him) come on foot and bring battle?’
segments.s221-g48: سر is ‘head’ (the object), سرکشان in context means ‘the rebellious’ or ‘recalcitrant ones’ (a plural substantive modifying whose heads are meant), زیر سنگ is a prepositional phrase ‘under a stone’ (زیر = beneath, سنگ = stone), and آورد is ‘brought/placed’. The clause asks if he did not place the rebels’ heads beneath a rock — a terse poetic phrasing without intervening particles.
segments.s221-g225: بگفت is an archaic past-tense verb meaning 'he said' (بـ- prefix + گفت). این سخن is the direct object 'these words/speech'. پیش خاقان چین is a prepositional/locative phrase 'in the presence of / before the Khaghan of China' (پیش = 'before/in the presence of', followed by the title خاقان and the place/name چین). Word order is verb → object → locative phrase, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s221-g226: همی is a classical particle indicating repeated or habitual action (often 'kept on / would'); گفت here functions as the verbal idea 'spoke/said' (the lexeme was given as a nominal sense list but functions verbally in context). با هر کسی means 'with/to each person' (با + هر + کسی = 'to everyone'), and همچنین 'in the same way / likewise' closes the clause. The clause literally reads 'he would say, to each person, likewise.'
segments.s221-g227: ز خورشید is a prepositional phrase 'from / because of the sun' (ز used poetically). چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when/as', شد is the past verb 'became', جهان is the subject 'the world', and لعل‌فام is the predicative adjective 'ruby-colored / ruby-hued'. The order places the time/causal phrase first, then the 'when became' construction followed by subject and predicate, typical of epic diction.
segments.s221-g228: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night'. بر چرخ is a locative phrase 'upon the sky/heaven' (بر = 'on/upon', چرخ = 'sky/heaven'). بگذاشت is the past verb 'he placed/put' (بـ- prefix + گذاشت), and گام is its object 'step/foot' — together 'placed its step' = 'set foot/step'. The verb precedes its object here, a common poetic order.
segments.s221-g229: دلیران لشکر is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the brave ones of the army' (the ezāfe is implicit in poetry). شدند is the past plural 'they became/turned into', and انجمن is the predicate noun 'an assembly / council'. So the structure is 'the army's brave men became an assembly.'
segments.s221-g230: که is a relative pronoun 'who/which' linking the previous noun to this clause. بودند is the past plural 'they were', followed by predicate adjectives/nouns دانا 'wise' and شمشیرزن 'swordsman' joined by و 'and'. The clause describes the assembly's members: 'who were wise and swordsmen.'
segments.s221-g231: به is the directional preposition 'to/towards', خرگاه is a locative noun (literally 'khar-gāh' — 'donkey-stall' or low place in the lexical list; here it names the place they go to), followed by خاقان چین 'the Khaghan of China' (title + place), and آمدند is the past plural 'they came'. The phrase is a simple motion clause: 'they came to [that place].'
segments.s221-g232: همه is 'all/everyone' and is the logical subject; دل پر از رزم و کین is a nominal phrase 'heart full of battle and vengeance' (دل used collectively for their hearts, پر از = 'full of', followed by the two nouns رزم and کین joined by و). آمدند repeats the past plural verb 'they came'. Note Persian often uses a singular noun like دل with a plural verb to express 'their hearts were full'.
segments.s221-g185: نه + برگیرد is a negative + verb construction: برگیرد is a 3rd-person-present/subjunctive-form of بر داشتن (‘take up, lift’), here used impersonally/passively to mean ‘be moved / be lifted’. از جای is a prepositional phrase ‘from his place’. گرزش is a possessive/nominal construction (گرز + -ش) meaning ‘his club/blow’, and نهنگ follows as the agent/epithet ‘the great one/whale’ — together ‘the whale’s club-blow’. Word order is poetic: prepositional phrase before the verb.
segments.s221-g186: اگر introduces a conditional clause. بفگند is an archaic/epic present-subjunctive form of فکندن (‘to throw, cast’) used here for a hypothetical (‘should throw’ or ‘if (it) is thrown’). بر زمین is the directional/place phrase ‘onto the ground’, and روز جنگ specifies time ‘on the day of battle’. The whole clause gives the counterfactual condition.
segments.s221-g187: زهی is an interjection/exclamation (‘ah!/oh!’). بر کمانش is a prepositional phrase ‘on his bow’ (کمان + possessive -ش). The sequence بر از چرم شیر is a poetic material phrase: از چرم شیر = ‘of lion leather’, with the preceding بر linking the covering/material to the bow. The line is elliptical (the copula is omitted) and praises the bow’s material.
segments.s221-g188: یکی here functions distributively/indefinitely (‘one’ / ‘a single’ or ‘each’). تیر و پیکان او is a coordinted possessive noun phrase ‘his arrows and shafts’ (او = his). The final phrase ده ستیر is hyperbolic: read as a numeric intensifier ‘ten strikers/warriors’ — i.e. ‘one arrow / dart of his is like ten warriors.’ The clause omits an explicit copula and uses poetic comparison.
segments.s221-g189: به رزم اندر آید is a fixed poetic sequence meaning ‘he goes into battle’ (به رزم = ‘to battle’, اندر آید = ‘enters’). بپوشد زره is a coordinated action: بپوشد (3rd-person-present/subjunctive/formal narrative) ‘he puts on’ followed by the direct object زره ‘armor’. The verbs appear in sequence to list the warrior’s actions.
segments.s221-g190: یکی introduces the item ‘a certain/one’ jošen (cuirass). از بر is a material/location phrase meaning ‘over/on (his body)’. ببندد is the 3rd-person verb ‘he fastens/ties’, and گره is the object ‘knot’. The verb follows the object in typical Persian order for emphasis: ‘he fastens a cuirass on and ties the knot.’
segments.s221-g191: یکی جامه دارد is ‘he has a certain garment’ (یکی = a/one). ز چرم پلنگ is a genitive/material phrase ‘of leopard leather’ (ز = poetic ‘of/from’). دارد is the simple present ‘has/possesses’ and links the noun to the warrior as possession.
segments.s221-g192: بپوشد بر is ‘he puts (it) on’ with بر indicating the surface/location. The conjunction و links the next verb اندر آید ‘he enters/comes in’, and به جنگ specifies destination ‘to the battle’. Two verbs are coordinated to describe successive actions.
segments.s221-g73: This clause means 'the arrow rained upon Rostam.' The prepositional phrase 'به رستم' (to/at Rostam) precedes the verb; 'بر آنگه' ('بر' + 'آنگه') adds the temporal/locative sense 'then/upon that,' and 'ببارید' is the past 3rd‑person singular of باریدن used metaphorically ('rained, fell'). The subject 'تیر' (arrow) follows the verb for poetic emphasis (verb before subject).
segments.s221-g74: 'تهمتن بدو گفت برخیره خیر' is a reporting clause: 'Tahmatan said to him, "for the good".' 'تهمتن' is the nominative subject; 'بدو' is the clitic dative 'to him'; 'گفت' functions as the past verb 'said' (here treated as speech verb in past simple); 'برخیره خیر' is an idiomatic phrase 'for the good/best' with 'برخیره' functioning adverbially and 'خیر' the noun 'good.' Word order is Subject – indirect object – verb – complement, typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s221-g75: 'همی رنجه داری تن خویش را' is a present/habitual statement: 'you continually endure/suffer (you) have distress.' 'همی' marks continuous or habitual action; 'رنجه' is a verbal noun/verb meaning 'suffer' used here with the auxiliary-like 'داری' (present 2nd person 'you have/you are having'); 'تن خویش را' is the definite object ('your own body') with 'خویش' split into 'خوی' + clitic 'ش' → 'his/own,' and 'را' marks definiteness/accusative.
segments.s221-g76: This coordinated noun phrase 'دو بازوی و جان بداندیش را' lists additional objects: 'the two arms and the life of the ill‑minded one.' 'دو بازوی' is 'two arms'; 'و' connects the nouns; 'جان' 'life' is followed by the postpositive adjective/descriptor 'بداندیش' ('ill‑minded, malicious'), and the final 'را' marks the whole phrase as definite/accusative. Note that adjectives or epithets often come after the noun in this style.
segments.s221-g77: 'تهمتن به بند کمر برد چنگ' describes an action: 'Tahmatan brought/took the grip (or held thing) to his belt.' 'تهمتن' is subject; 'به بند کمر' is a prepositional phrase 'to/at the band of the waist' (i.e., to his belt); 'برد' is the past 3rd‑person singular of بردن 'to take/carry' (here rendered as 'took/brought') though that specific sense form was not supplied in the senses list; 'چنگ' is the direct object (here 'grip/handle' or similar) placed after the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s221-g78: 'گزین کرد یک چوبه تیر خدنگ' is a single clause 'he chose one shaft of a dagger‑arrow.' The verb is the compound past 'گزین کرد' (گزین + auxiliary کرد) = 'chose/selected'; 'یک' precedes the noun 'چوبه' (a beam/shaft) as the numeral; 'تیر خدنگ' is a genitive/attributive construction where 'خدنگ' postmodifies 'تیر' ('a dagger‑type arrow'). Word order is Verb – object phrase (poetic freedom) but the meaning is straightforward.
segments.s221-g79: 'یکی تیر الماس، پیکان چو آب' is a descriptive apposition: 'one diamond arrow, the shaft like water.' 'یکی تیر الماس' = 'one (arrow) of diamond' (numeric + noun + attributive), and 'پیکان چو آب' is a simile 'the shaft/arrow like water,' where 'چو' introduces the comparison ('like/as'). The clauses are compressed for poetic effect.
segments.s221-g80: 'نهاده بر او چار پر عقاب' gives a result/state: 'four eagle‑feathers were placed upon it.' 'نهاده' is the perfect/past participle (placed/laid); 'بر او' = 'on it'; 'چار' here is the numeral 'four' modifying 'پر' ('feather'); and 'عقاب' is the specifying noun 'eagle.' The participle 'نهاده' governs the following prepositional phrase to describe how the arrow was equipped.
segments.s221-g81: This line uses the classical Persian (verb–subject–object or V S O) flexibility: بمالید (rubbed) is the past tense verb and رستم (Rostam) is the subject that follows it for emphasis. کمان را is the definite object marked by را. به چنگ is an instrumental/locative phrase meaning “in/with his grip” (چنگ here = talon/hand in this martial context) and follows the verb phrase as a circumstantial adjunct.
segments.s221-g82: This clause continues with a past‑tense action: اندر آورد (a two‑word perfective phrase built on آورد, “bring/put”) means “brought/placed into/onto.” به شست is a directional/locative phrase meaning “onto/to the thumb” (شست = thumb). تیر خدنگ is a compound object (tīr = arrow; khadang = a short dagger‑like head or small dagger) naming the projectile he places.
segments.s221-g83: Poetic, compact sequence of actions: خم کرد and کرد are past‑tense verbs (“made/bent, made/did”) describing successive motions. راست and چپ mark directions (right / left). The initial برو is an archaic or poetic motion marker here (literally “go”/“move”), used to introduce the movement of the bow or string; word order is compressed for meter, so direction adverbs appear adjacent to the verbs they modify.
segments.s221-g84: Simple past clause: خروش (a roar/cry) is the subject and بخاست is the past tense verb “arose/rose up.” از خم marks origin/source “from the curve/bend.” چرخ here is a concrete image (“wheel” or circular frame) used metaphorically with خم; چاچی is an unusual/archaic lexical item (its exact sense is debated or dialectal) and appears here as an attributive or echoic element modifying or accompanying چرخ. The whole phrase means that a loud sound issued from the bow’s (or mechanism’s) curve.
segments.s221-g85: چو introduces a temporal clause “when.” سوفارش is a proper name (the thing or beast approaching). آمد is the past tense “came.” به پهنای گوش is an idiomatic locative: literally “to the width of an ear,” i.e. within earshot or within the span of hearing; note the post‑verbal placement of the locative phrase, common in poetry.
segments.s221-g86: This is a straightforward past result clause: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source “from,” شاخ گوزنان is a genitive construction “the antlers/horns of the deer (guzan = stag/deer),” and برآمد (past of برآمدن) is the verb “arose/issued forth.” خروش is the thing that arose (a cry/roar). Word order places the source phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s221-g87: چو introduces a temporal clause “when/as.” بوسید (here in the sense “touched lightly”) governs the object پیکان (the arrow). سرانگشت اوی is a possessive phrase “his fingertip” where اوی is an archaic possessive pronoun (“his”). The verb is applied to the arrow as the subject in this clause (poetic anthropomorphizing).
segments.s221-g88: گذر کرد is a compound meaning “passed” (گذر + کرد = passed/made a passage). بر is the preposition “over/on,” مهره پشت اوی is a genitive phrase “the vertebra (marginally 'mohreh' = joint/vertebra) of his back,” with اوی again the archaic possessive “his.” The clause states the trajectory: the arrow passed over a vertebra on his back.
segments.s221-g145: ببینید is a 2nd-person plural imperative (or polite imperative) 'look/consider.' تا here introduces the purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that') and links to the subordinate question phrase. چاره کار چیست places the interrogative چی + copula ست at the end (classical word order) and literally asks 'what is the remedy of the affair.'
segments.s221-g146: بران is an archaic/prepositional item meaning 'from/for that' or 'as for those,' referring back to something just mentioned. The clause has the prepositional phrase بر آزار ('upon/for harm') followed by the interrogative چی and the copula ست at the end—again the classical order—so the whole asks 'and what is the harm upon/caused by those fatigues?'
segments.s221-g147: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus said;' the speaker's name follows (کاموس). کامروز is a temporal adverb 'this very day' modifying جنگ ('battle') — classical Persian often places the time-word directly before the noun it modifies. Note: گفت here is the perfect/past reporting verb 'said.'
segments.s221-g148: This is a result clause introduced by چنان ... که ('so ... that'). بد is the predicate 'bad/ill'; که introduces the consequence/result clause. نام اندر آمد به ننگ uses اندر آمد 'came into' and the phrase به ننگ 'into shame/disgrace' — literally 'the name came into shame,' i.e., the reputation was disgraced.
segments.s221-g149: به رزم اندرون is a locative/temporal prepositional phrase 'in/at the inner battle.' کشته شد is a passive construction ('was killed'); کشته is the past participle and شد is the auxiliary marking the passive/result. اشکبوس is the name of the slain man.
segments.s221-g150: وزو is an archaic contracted form (= وز او / از او) meaning 'because of him' or 'from him.' شادمان شد is 'became joyful' (شد marks the change of state). دل گیو و طوس is a possessed noun phrase 'the hearts of Giv and Tūs' (possessor follow the noun in Persian); together: 'the hearts of Giv and Tūs became joyful.'
segments.s221-g151: دلم is 'my heart' (دل + enclitic possessive -م). زان is an archaic prepositional/causal 'from that/for that reason' referring to the preceding person; پیاده here means 'infantry/foot-soldier.' به دو نیم شد is a result phrase 'became into two halves' (شد marks the resulting change), i.e. 'was split in two.'
segments.s221-g152: کزو is a classical relative/prepositional pronoun 'from whom / from which' referring back to that person; لشکر ما is 'our army' (noun + possessive pronoun). پر از بیم شد is a copular/result clause 'became full of fear' (پر از = 'full of', شد = 'became').
segments.s221-g217: بسي (بسی) here is an intensive adverb meaning ‘much/very’ modifying the following noun آفرین. آفرین is an exclamation/noun ‘praise, acclamation’ and خواند is a past tense verb ‘read/uttered/voiced’. The subject پیران بدوی (literally ‘the ancient/primeval elders’) follows the verb in typical classical Persian V‑S‑O order; بدوی is an epithet attached to پیران.
segments.s221-g218: که‌ای is a vocative particle here (classical 'که' + 'ای') used to call/address someone: ‘O…’. شاه is the vocative noun ‘king’. بینادل is a poetic epithet (uncertain/rare reading) applied to the king, and و connects it to راست‌گوی, an adjective meaning ‘truth‑speaking/truthful’. The whole phrase is the elders’ direct address to the king.
segments.s221-g219: بدین is effectively the compound ‘به + این’ and introduces the list of body parts; poetic spelling splits it here. The sequence شاخ و این یال و بازوی و کفت is a right‑branching list (‘this horn and this mane and arm and palm’) with و (‘and’) joining items; شاخ, یال, بازو and کفت are concrete nouns (horn, mane, arm, palm).
segments.s221-g220: هنرمند باشی is a verbless clause with هنرمند (skillful) followed by باشی, a classical subjunctive/present form meaning ‘you be / you are (may you be)’. ندارم شگفت is literal ‘I do not have wonder’ → idiomatically ‘I am not surprised’. The negative verb ندارم takes the preceding verbless clause as its content: ‘I have no wonder that you are skillful.’
segments.s221-g221: به کام تو گردد is a classical optative/jussive construction: به کام تو ‘to/for your desire’ + گردد (subjunctive/optative of شدن/گشتن) meaning ‘may it become / may it turn out’. همه کار ما is the object (‘all our affairs’). Word order is prepositional phrase + verb + object, typical in high/poetic Persian.
segments.s221-g222: نماندست is an archaic/perhaps contracted verbal form (negative construction around ماندن) used here to indicate that the fighting is not finished / still remains; in older Persian such negatives can carry an idiomatic sense of ‘has not ceased / is not left’. بسیار پیکار ما is a noun phrase ‘much/many of our fighting’ placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s221-g223: وزان جایگه is a prepositional/time phrase ‘from that place/spot’. گرد لشکر بگشت is a verb group meaning ‘the army turned about/returned’ (گرد here gives a sense of ‘around/about’ and بگشت is a past action ‘turned/returned’). The subject ('لشکر') follows the adverbial phrase, typical in epic diction.
segments.s221-g224: به هر خیمه و پرده‌ای is a distributive phrase ‘to/round each tent and curtain’ (هر = each). برگذشت is the past verb ‘passed by/passed over’. The suffix -ای on پرده‌ای is an indefinite/poetic marker (‘a curtain’/‘each curtain’), and the clause describes the army/leader moving from tent to tent.
segments.s221-g97: نگه کرد is a past-tense verbal phrase (نگه‌کردن = to look, to fix one's gaze); Ferdowsi places the verb before its subjects here (poetic inversion). کاموس و خاقان چین is the compound subject: کاموس (a proper name) coordinated with خاقان چین (‘the khaghan of China’), linked by و. Together they form ‘Kamus and the Chinese khaghan’ as the agents of نگه کرد.
segments.s221-g98: بر introduces the prepositional object of the gaze (‘upon/on’). آن is the demonstrative determiner 'that/the'. برز و بالا are paired nouns/adjectives meaning ‘exaltation/highness’ and ‘loftiness’; و simply coordinates them. زور و کین is another coordinated noun pair ‘strength/force and hatred/vengeance’ and completes the prepositional phrase receiving نگه کرد.
segments.s221-g99: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction meaning 'when'. برگشت is the simple past 'returned/turned back'. رستم is the proper name subject, and هم 'also' indicates simultaneity. اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'in/at the time' = 'at that moment', giving temporal setting for the following action.
segments.s221-g100: سواری is the direct object or indefinite noun 'a rider/horseman'. فرستاد is the past tense 'sent' (verb before the subject in this poetic line). خاقان is the subject 'the khaghan'. The final token دمان is a proper name in apposition (the rider's name), set off by the comma; the sequence means 'the khaghan sent a rider, Doman.'
segments.s221-g101: کزان is an archaic compound (کِ زآن) meaning 'from that/from him' and introduces the source. نامور is an epithet 'the famed/renowned one' modifying the implied agent. تیر is the object 'arrow'. بیرون کشید is a light-verb combination meaning 'drew out' (بیرون + کشید); word order places the object before the verb in this SOV style.
segments.s221-g102: همه تیر = 'every/ all the arrows' (همه as a quantifier before the noun). تا here means 'as far as' (تا پر = 'to the feather'). The first پر is the noun 'feather' (the fletching); the second پر is adjectival 'full (of)'. از introduces the content of fullness ('of'), so پر از خون = 'feather full of blood'. کشید repeats the verb 'drew/pulled' (i.e., the arrows came away soaked).
segments.s221-g103: همه لشکر is the subject 'the whole army/host'. آن تیر is the direct object 'those arrows' (آن as demonstrative). برداشتند is the past plural verb 'they picked up/took up', agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s221-g104: سراسر همه is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'throughout/all everywhere'. نیزه is the noun 'spears'. پنداشتند is the past plural of پنداشتن here meaning 'they imagined/assumed', so the line says that everywhere they supposed the shafts to be spears (i.e., they mistook the bloody arrows for spears).
segments.s221-g241: برفتند is a past plural verb ('they departed') placed before the following noun phrase in poetic word order. هر کس is an indefinite distributive phrase 'each person' (هر + کس). به آرام خویش is a prepositional phrase 'to/for his own ease/rest' where آرام is the noun/adjective 'calm/rest' and خویش + enclitic ش marks the possessive 'his own'; the phrase modifies the motion expressed by برفتند.
segments.s221-g242: بخفتند is the past plural of خوابیدن 'they slept'. در خیمه is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the tent(s)'. با کام خویش is another prepositional phrase 'with his own کام' where کام here means 'satisfaction/fulfillment' and خویش + ش is the reflexive/possessive 'his own'; the whole clause states the result/state of the people after departing.
segments.s221-g243: چو here functions temporally 'when/as'. باریک و خمیده شد is a compound predicate: باریک 'thin/slender' and خمیده 'bent' linked by و, followed by شد 'became' (past), so 'became thin and bent'. پشت ماه is the noun phrase 'the moon's back' (پشت = back, ماه = moon) serving as the subject of شد.
segments.s221-g244: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. تاریک‌زلف is a compound noun 'dark tresses' (تاریک + زلف) describing the source. شبان سیاه is a noun phrase where شبان (here 'youths' or 'young people') is qualified by the adjective سیاه 'black/dark'; together 'the dark-tressed black youths'—the phrase gives the cause/source for the moon's change.
segments.s221-g245: به نزدیک خورشید is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near the sun' indicating proximity. چون شد درست is a clause where چون functions 'as/when' and شد درست means 'became right/clear' (شد = became, درست = proper/exact) describing a change when approaching the sun.
segments.s221-g246: برآمد is an intransitive past verb 'arose/appeared'. پر از آب is a phrase 'full of water' (پر functioning adjectivally with از marking the 'of' relation). رخ را بشست is a coordinated outcome: رخ را (face + definite object marker را) and بشست 'washed' — the implied subject of بشست is the thing which برآمد (i.e., what arose and then washed the face). The clause shows an implied subject carried across two verbs, a common poetic economy.
segments.s221-g247: سپاه دو کشور is a genitive-like construction meaning 'the army of the two countries/realms' (noun followed by its qualifier). برآمد is the past 'arose/rose', and به جوش is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in a boil/surge' (جوش = boiling/surge), so the whole means the army surged up in violent excitement.
segments.s221-g248: به چرخ بلند is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the high sky' (چرخ = sky/heaven, بلند = high). اندر آمد is a compound where اندر 'into/in' plus آمد 'came' yields 'came into' and خروش is the noun 'roar/uproar' that is presented as having come — the verb precedes the noun in this poetic inversion: 'into the high sky came a roar.'
segments.s221-g1: دلیری کجا is an exclamatory interrogative phrase (literary) meaning “what/what a bravery!”; Persian often uses کجا in older poetry as an exclamation. نام او اشکبوس is an equative nominal clause with the copula omitted (i.e. “his name [was] Ashkabus”); the order 'نام او اشکبوس' places the name after 'نام او' without a linking verb.
segments.s221-g2: همی marks continuous or iterative aspect (poetic), so the verb expresses repeated or ongoing action: “kept/was continually.” بر خروشید is the past-form of خروشیدن with the preposition بر as an intensifier ‘raised a cry/roared upon’. بر سان کوس is a simile: بر + سان (سان = “like/as”) + کوس = “roared like a drum” or “sounded like a war‑drum.”
segments.s221-g3: بیامد is a past narrative verb “he came.” که here marks purpose (“in order that / to” — classical usage). جوید is the present/subjunctive root meaning “seek” used with purpose: “came to seek.” ز ایران نبرد = “battle from Iran” (ز = from). The clause as a whole: “he came in order to seek battle from Iran.”
segments.s221-g4: سر here is the literal anatomical/decapitation sense “head.” هم‌نبرد is the compound meaning “comrade‑in‑arms” (a fellow warrior). اندر آرد = ‘bring into/bring within’ (آرد = brings; اندر = in/into). به گرد: به + گرد indicates motion/taking to the midst/around; گرد here carries the sense ‘around/about / midst’ (i.e. ‘into the ranks’). The string means: “(he) brings the head of his comrade‑in‑arms into the midst (of the host).”
segments.s221-g5: بشد is a classical past-form meaning “he became / it came to be.” تیز qualifies as “swift” here. رهام is the subject (proper name). با خود و گبر lists companions/associates: با = “with,” خود = “himself / his own (i.e. with him),” و = conjunction “and,” گبر names the companion (literally “gabr,” here a person described as a Zoroastrian/infidel). The phrase therefore says Roham became swift and (with him) a gabr was present.
segments.s221-g6: همی again marks iterative/continuous aspect (“kept / was repeatedly”). گرد رزم here means ‘around/about the battle’ or ‘circling the field’ (گرد = around/about; رزم = battle). اندر آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning “entered / came into” (اندر = into/in; آمد = came). به ابر is a locative/simile ‘on/like a cloud’ (به + ابر = ‘like a cloud’ or ‘upon a cloud’).
segments.s221-g7: برآویخت is a poetic past verb meaning “rose up / leapt up.” رهام is the subject, and با اشکبوس indicates accompaniment: ‘with Ashkabus’ (no copula is needed). The verb + companion phrase conveys that Roham and Ashkabus sprang up together.
segments.s221-g8: برآمد is a past verb “arose / issued forth.” ز هر دو سپه = “from both hosts/armies” (ز = from; هر دو = both; سپه = host/army). بوق و کوس lists the instruments that sounded: بوق = horn/trumpet, و = and, کوس = war‑drum. The clause means that trumpets and drums rose from both armies.
segments.s221-g233: چو here is a comparative/enumerative introducer meaning 'such as / like', starting a list. کاموس and اسپ‌افگن are presented in apposition (names or epithets); Persian allows juxtaposition of names/epithets without linking particles. شیرمرد follows them as a descriptive noun ('brave man/hero') that applies to the preceding names.
segments.s221-g234: The second چو repeats the same comparative introducer ('such as'), linking a new pair of names/epithets. منشور و فرطوس are two coordinated names/terms (و = 'and'), and مرد نبرد is a nominal phrase in which مرد ('man/hero') is qualified by نبرد ('of battle'), i.e. 'men of battle' or 'warriors'. Word order is noun + modifier (مرد + نبرد) as an attributive phrase.
segments.s221-g235: This chunk lists place-names or epithets: شمیران followed by شگنی and شنگل coordinated by و. The preposition ز ('from') marks origin and governs هند ('India') at the end; in Persian poetry the preposition can appear later in the line while still applying to the preceding names.
segments.s221-g236: ز سقلاب marks another origin ('from Ṣaqlāb' — an obscure/place name). چون here means 'like/as' and introduces a simile comparing something to کندر ('frankincense'). The conjunction و links this simile to شاه سند ('the king of Sind'), where شاه (king) modifies سند (Sind), a compound name phrase.
segments.s221-g237: همی is the continuous/iterative particle (often used in epic past) meaning 'kept on / would'. رای زد is an idiomatic collocation: رای ('counsel/plan') is the object and زد (the verb 'to strike') here means 'to give/offer' — together 'to give counsel' or 'to propose a plan'. رزم را uses را as the definite object marker ('the battle'), and هر کسی ('each person') is the subject phrase.
segments.s221-g238: از ایران سخن گفت is 'spoke about Iran' where از marks the topic/source ('concerning Iran'), سخن ('words/speech') is the object of گفت (spoke). هر کس بسی is a closing clause meaning 'each person (هر کس) as much as he could / sufficiently (بسی)', with بسی functioning as an adverb 'enough' or 'a great deal'.
segments.s221-g239: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that'. بر آن رایشان شد درست is a conventional construction: بر آن (upon that) + رای‌شان (their decision/opinion) + شد (became) + درست (right/proper) — 'their decision became fixed/correct on that.' Note رایشان is رای + شان (their).
segments.s221-g240: که introduces the content of the decision ('that'). یک‌سر here is an adverb 'entirely/completely'; به خون is a prepositional phrase 'in/with blood' (به + خون). دست بایست شست is a semi-idiomatic modal construction: بایست ('ought/must') governs the verbal action شست (the past form of شستن used here as an infinitive/gerund 'wash'), so literally 'one must wash the hand' = 'one must (completely) wash one's hands in blood' (i.e., avenge with bloodshed).
segments.s221-g209: 'ببین' is the imperative 'look/see'. 'تا' introduces the subordinate content clause ('so that/…what'), here meaning 'what' you will ask; 'چه' is the interrogative 'what', and 'خواهی' is 2nd-person present ('you will / you desire'). 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from', governing 'سوگند' ('oath'), and 'سخت' is an adjective 'harsh/severe' modifying 'سوگند'. Word order places the object phrase 'ز سوگند سخت' after the verb phrase.
segments.s221-g210: Here 'که' is the relative/complementizer 'that' linking the clause to the preceding noun 'سوگند'. 'خوردند' is the classical use of خوردن with abstract acts (here idiomatically 'to take/swear' an oath) — chosen from the available senses closest to 'befall/experience'. 'شاهان' is plural 'kings' and 'بیداربخت' is the adjective 'ill‑fated/unhappy' describing those kings. The clause literally says 'which ill‑fated kings [خوردند]'.
segments.s221-g211: The verb 'خورم' is first‑person singular and here used optatively/subjunctively ('may it befall me' / 'may I receive'), not literal 'to eat'. 'من' is the emphatic subject 'I'. 'فزون' means 'more' or 'additional'. 'زان' = 'of that/from that', 'کنون' = 'now', and 'پیش تو' = 'in your presence' (پیش here 'in front of / before'). The order places circumstances ('کنون پیش تو') after the quantified object phrase.
segments.s221-g212: 'که' introduces the purposive/result clause 'so that…'. 'روشن' here means 'become enlightened/clear' and 'شود' is the intransitive auxiliary/verb 'become' (3rd person subjunctive/optative). 'زان' again 'from that', and 'دل و کیش تو' = 'your heart and your creed/faith' (دل = heart; کیش = creed). The clause expresses purpose/result: 'so that that your heart and faith may be enlightened.'
segments.s221-g213: 'که' again introduces a subordinate clause ('that…'). 'زین' is 'saddle' and 'را' is the definite object marker. 'نه بردارم' is negated first‑person present/subjunctive ('that I not remove/take off'), with 'بردارم' from برداشتن 'to take/remove'. 'از پشت' = 'from the back of' and 'بور' describes the possessor as 'fair‑haired' (i.e., 'the fair one'). The clause literally: 'that I do not remove this saddle from the fair one's back.'
segments.s221-g214: 'به' here marks the instrument or cause 'by/with'. 'نیروی' is 'force/strength' and 'یزدان' is the divine name 'Yezdān' (God). 'کیوان' (Kayvān) and 'هور' (Hūr) are invoked as divine/cosmic powers; 'و' links them. The prepositional phrase gives the condition/instrument: 'by the power of Yezdān, Kayvān and Hūr.'
segments.s221-g215: 'مگر' is functioning as 'unless' (introducing a conditional/exceptive clause). 'بخت' is 'fortune/luck' and 'رای' here means 'counsel/plan' (not merely 'opinion'). 'تو' is the possessive 'your'. 'روشن کنم' is a causative/active phrase 'I make clear' (کنم = first‑person present subjunctive/optative used for volitional/intentional action). Together: 'Unless I make your fortune and counsel clear…'.
segments.s221-g216: The fronted phrase 'بر ایشان جهان' places the action 'before them, in the world' (بر = 'upon/before', ایشان = 'them', جهان = 'the world'). The verb phrase 'چشم سوزن کنم' literally 'I make (their) eye a needle' — a vivid idiom here meaning to prick/burn/punish their eyes with a needle; 'کنم' is again 1st‑person subjunctive used to state the intended action. The unusual word order (circumstantial phrase before verb) is typical of epic/poetic Persian.
segments.s221-g113: همی is an emphatic/continuous particle placed before the verb; خوار کردی is a past (2nd singular) verbal phrase where کردی (you did) is the finite verb and خوار (made low/despised) functions as the resultive/complement meaning 'made contemptible'. سراسر سخن is the direct object (entire speech) placed after the verb — classical Persian allows verb before object.
segments.s221-g114: جز آن introduces an exception ('except that'); بد here qualifies that 'thing' ('that bad thing'); که begins a relative clause ('which/that'); گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' (introducing what was said); ز سر تا به بن is a fixed prepositional phrase 'from head to root/top to bottom' specifying extent.
segments.s221-g115: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (indirect object) placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the verb 'said' (classical past); پیران is the subject 'the elders'; کز ایران سپاه is a subordinate/relative phrase 'that/from the Iranian host' which will be continued — word order is inverted (indirect object + verb + subject).
segments.s221-g116: ندانم is 'I do not know' (negated present/past sense used as main comment); کسی را is the object with را marking the person (here 'anyone'); بدین پایگاه is a classical prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at this position/base' (بدین = 'in this' + پایگاه = 'stronghold/base'). The clause means 'I know of no one in this position/base.'
segments.s221-g117: کجا is the interrogative/adverb 'where' placed at the clause start; تیر او is the subject+possessor 'his arrow'; بگذرد is the present/subjunctive form 'passes/will pass' (classical use after question), and بر درخت is a prepositional phrase 'against/on the tree' indicating target.
segments.s221-g118: This clause begins again with ندانم 'I do not know'; چه دارد is a compact interrogative-object 'what (it) has/contains' (چه = what; دارد = he/it has); به دل شوربخت is a locative/possessive phrase 'in the wretched heart' giving the locus of that 'what'. The construction places the question-word چه before the verb as object.
segments.s221-g119: از ایرانیان is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the Iranians' setting the group; گیو و طوس‌اند is a coordinated subject 'Giv and Tous' with the archaic plural copula -اند attached (they are); مرد is the predicate noun 'men/heroes' placed after the copula — classical Persian often places the predicate at sentence end.
segments.s221-g120: که is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking back to مرد; با فر is an instrumental phrase 'with (divine) glory' (فر = royal/divine glory); و links to برزند which is an archaic/plutal present meaning 'come/appear' (verb agrees with the plural subject); روز نبرد is an adverbial time phrase 'on the day of battle'. The whole relative clause modifies 'men'.
segments.s221-g17: برآهیخت is a past-tense, literary verb meaning “rose up” or “sprang up,” placed before the subject in the poetic inversion. رهام is the subject (proper name) following the verb. گرز گران is a noun phrase ‘heavy mace’ placed after the verb–subject clause; in classical Persian the weapon phrase often follows without a possessive marker, implying ‘his heavy mace’ or the mace he wielded.
segments.s221-g18: غمی شد is a light-predicative construction: شد (became/occurred) with غمی (a grief/sorrow) — a poetic way to say ‘grief arose’ or ‘grief befell.’ ز پیکار is a prepositional phrase using poetic ز = ‘from/because of’ to mark cause (‘because of the battle’). دست سران is a juxtaposition meaning ‘the hands of the chiefs/leaders’ (دست + modifier سران), functioning as the affected party (‘befell the leaders’).
segments.s221-g19: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ رهام is the subject. گشت is the past tense of گشتن (‘became/turned’) used here as ‘became’ or ‘grew.’ از + کشانی uses ز/از to mark cause/sourceness and کشانی (a verbal noun from کشاند) means ‘the dragging/being hauled about’; ستوه is a predicative adjective ‘weary/sick of.’ Together: ‘when Roham grew weary from the dragging.’
segments.s221-g20: بپیچید is a past form meaning ‘he turned’ (he turned aside). زو is the prepositional contraction of ز + او here meaning ‘from it/him’ (away from that/it). روی (‘face’) is the noun used with پیچیدن to mean ‘turn one’s face’ (i.e. turn away). و شد سوی کوه links with و = ‘and/then’ and شد (here an inchoative/light verb) + سوی کوه ‘toward the mountain’ to indicate motion ‘and he went/turned toward the mountain.’
segments.s221-g21: ز قلب سپاه uses poetic ز = ‘from/because of’ + قلب سپاه ‘the heart (center) of the army/host’ to mark source or location; اندر آشفت is a verbal phrase where اندر = ‘in/within’ and آشفت (past) = ‘was distressed/was thrown into confusion.’ طوس (Tūs) is the subject placed at the end in this poetic word order: ‘Tūs was disturbed within the army’s heart.’
segments.s221-g22: بزد is the simple past ‘he struck’ (or ‘he smote/charged’). اسپ is ‘steed/horse’ as the instrument or vehicle. کاید appears here in immediate apposition to اسپ (most likely a name of the horse) and does not fit the provided lexical senses for کاید (so it is treated as a name in this line). بر is the preposition ‘against/at’ introducing the target, and اشکبوس is the enemy’s name ‘Ashkabus.’ The sequence reads: ‘he struck (or charged) with the steed Kayd against Ashkabus.’
segments.s221-g23: تهمتن is an epic epithet (here the subject). برآشفت is a prefixed past verb meaning ‘became angry’ or ‘was enraged’ (بر- plus آشفت). و با طوس گفت links him to Tūs: و = ‘and,’ با = ‘with,’ طوس = ‘Tūs.’ گفت in context functions as ‘said/spoke,’ but the provided sense list does not include the verb sense, so it is used here as the reporting verb ‘he said (to Tūs).’
segments.s221-g24: که is the complementizer ‘that’ introducing reported speech. رهام را uses the object/topic marker را after the name Roham to mark him as the topic of the following clause. جام باده‌ست is a nominal phrase + copula: جام ‘cup’ (here ‘cup’/‘draught’) + باده‌ست ‘is wine’ (bāde‑st = ‘it is wine’). جفت is a predicate noun meaning ‘pair/mate’ — so the clause asserts ‘that Roham’s mate/pair is a cup of wine.’
segments.s221-g169: چگونست is an archaic contraction of چگون + است meaning “how is” and introduces a question about state or quality. مردی is the noun “man” (here “what sort of man”), followed by و “and” linking a second question; دیدار + اوی functions as a noun + possessive pronoun (“his appearance/visage”), so the clause asks about both the man and his looks.
segments.s221-g170: چگونه begins the interrogative “how”; شوم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of شدن “to become/ fare” meaning “shall I become / how shall I fare”; من is the explicit subject “I”; به + پیکار marks motion/contest into battle (“into/against battle”); اوی is the third‑person object/pronoun “him” (here the opponent). The whole clause asks how the speaker will do fighting that man.
segments.s221-g171: گر is the conditional “if”; ایدونک is a small adverbial/particle (here giving a sense of “perhaps / by chance / so”) used in Ferdowsi’s diction; اویست here refers to the name Owais (اویس) — a proper name / epithet — and کامد is the archaic present/past form of آمدن “to come”; ز راه is the prepositional phrase “from the road / by the way.” Together they form the protasis (if‑clause).
segments.s221-g172: This clause gives the result: (مرا) را رفت باید به آوردگاه. را is the definite/object/topic marker; رفت (here infinitival/old form of رفتن/رفتن) with باید (“must / it is necessary”) yields “must go”; به آوردگاه = “to the battlefield.” The implied agent is “me” (مرا), so the sense is “I must go to the field.”
segments.s221-g173: بدو ‘to him’ is the indirect object (to/unto him); گفت here is the past‑tense verb “said” (though the provided lexical tag lists noun senses, in context it is the verb گفتن); پیران is the subject “the elders”; که introduces the reported clause; این خود مباد is an optative/negative wish: این خود (“this itself”) + مباد (“may it not be”). In short: “The elders said to him: ‘May this not be.’”
segments.s221-g174: This is the content of the elders’ warning: که introduces “that”; او “he”; آید is the present/subjunctive of آمدن “come” (here expressing potential/hoped‑against action); ایدر “here”; کند is the verb “do/make” (used with the following noun); رزم یاد is a verbal phrase meaning “to take up / practise / wage battle” (یاد with کند = to commence or perform the act). So the clause means “that he come here to make war.”
segments.s221-g175: یکی here means “a certain / someone”; مرد is “man” or “hero”; بینی is the 2nd‑person present of دیدن “you see” (an address to the hearer/reader: “you see”); چو = “like/as”; سرو = “cypress” (a poetic image for tall graceful stature); سهی is a poetic epithet ‘dark/black’ or ornamental adjective modifying سرو. The line uses a simile (“like a cypress”) to describe the man.
segments.s221-g176: به دیدار = literally “in/with regard to appearance” or “to the sight”; با زیب و با فرهی = “with beauty and with nobility/cultured bearing,” where با is the preposition “with,” زیب is “beauty/adornment,” و is “and,” and فرهی is a poetic adjective meaning cultured/noble. Together: a visual description stressing both handsome looks and noble bearing.
segments.s221-g281: که introduces this subordinate/declarative clause ('that/for'); من رخش را بستم is a simple past-predicate: من (subject) + رخش را (object marked by را) + بستم (past 'I tied/shod'). امروز is a temporal adverb 'today' that precedes the following noun نعل; the noun phrase امروز نعل is left open here and its verb continues in the next segment.
segments.s221-g282: نعل به خون کرد is a causative/result clause: نعل (horseshoe) + به خون (into/with blood) + کرد (make/do). In this poetic line the auxiliary/tense element خواهم (I will) appears after کرد (an inversion common in poetry), so کرد + خواهم together express a future action 'I will make (it) bloody'. سر تیغ، لعل is an elliptic/nominal phrase appositive/metaphor: 'head (of the) sword — (like a) ruby', where لعل (ruby/deep red) describes the gleam/colour (metaphorically of a head made bloody).
segments.s221-g283: بسازید is the plural imperative/optative of ساختن ('make/prepare'); کامروز is an emphatic temporal adverb 'this very day'; روز نوست is a nominal clause with the older/poetic copula -ست appended to نو (new) meaning 'the day is new'. Word order places the verb 'بسازید' at the start (imperative) and the predicative adjective after روز with the archaic copula.
segments.s221-g284: زمین سر به سر is an idiomatic phrase 'the earth throughout / from end to end' (سر به سر functioning adverbially 'all over'); گنج کیخسروست is a possessive/nominal statement where گنج (treasure) is followed by the possessor Ki-Khosrow expressed inside کیخسروست (the name plus poetic copula '-ست' meaning 'is [of] Kay Khosrow' / 'belongs to Kay Khosrow').
segments.s221-g285: میان را ببندید is an imperative (ببندید, 2nd-person plural) with میان (middle/centre) as the object marked by را: 'close the centre'. کز is a relative/causal particle contracted from 'که از' and here introduces the following clause 'from the battle' or 'so that from the battlefield...'; کارزار (battlefield) is its complement.
segments.s221-g286: همه تاج یابید is optative/imperative-subjunctive (یابید, 2nd-person plural subjunctive/imperative-form) meaning 'may you all obtain/find crowns' (همه 'all' is subject/agent scope). با گوشوار is an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with earrings' specifying how/with what the crowns are obtained or with what ornament the crowns appear.
segments.s221-g287: بزرگان (the great ones/nobles) is the subject. برو here is a short poetic particle/adverbial usage (not the literal motion 'go') placed before خواندند to give a colloquial/poetic tone ('then/thereupon' or 'toward him'); خواندند is past plural 'they cried/sang/called' and governs the quoted/objec­tive آفرین ('Bravo!'), i.e. 'the nobles cried "Bravo!".'
