segments.s221-g288: که introduces the content/reason clause 'that/because'; از تو is a prepositional phrase 'because of you / from you' (literal 'from you'); فروزد is the present/future-looking verb 'shines/kindles' (poetic present) meaning 'will make shine' or 'will shine', taking کلاه و نگین ('cap and gem') as its objects — i.e. 'the cap and the jewel will shine because of you.'
segments.s221-g177: بسا رزمگاها که افراسیاب — بسا (an adverbial quantifier) modifies the plural noun رزمگاها, giving 'many battlefields'; که introduces a relative clause ('where/that') and افراسیاب is the subject of that relative clause. Word order is noun phrase + که + subject as is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s221-g178: ازو گشت پیچان و دیده پرآب — ازو is a pronominal prepositional form 'from/ because of him/it' introducing the cause or source; گشت is the past/quotative sense of 'become/turn' (here 'became' or 'turned'); پیچان is an adjectival/participial description 'twisting/distressed' describing Afrasiab's state; و links the clause to the following nominal phrase; دیده پرآب is a kenning-like phrase 'eye(s) full of water' = teary-eyed. The sequence shows an archaic/poetic use of گشت + participle/adjective to mean 'became X'.
segments.s221-g179: یکی رزمسازست و خسروپرست — یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one/someone' (a certain type). رزمساز + است (written fused as رزمسازست, an archaic poetic copula) is a predicate nominal 'is a war-maker'; و links the second predicate خسروپرست 'royal-flatterer/sycophant'. This is a simple nominal clause with an archaic fused copula.
segments.s221-g180: نخست او برد سوی شمشیر دست — نخست is an adverb 'first/initially'; او is the subject 'he'; برد here is the past meaning 'took/brought' (in the lexicon choices closest to 'gain' but functionally here a past verb 'he took'); سوی + شمشیر دست is an adverbial phrase 'to/toward the sword's hand' (= 'took up the sword'), with قلمرو شعری (poetic) word order where the object/goal follows سوی. The verb is simple past; the phrasing is idiomatic 'بَرِد سویِ شمشیرِ دست' = 'took up the sword'.
segments.s221-g181: به کین سیاوش کند کارزار — به کین 'for/by vengeance' (به = 'for' here) gives the motive; سیاوش is the subject; کند is the present/habitual (classical narrative) form of 'do/make' meaning 'wages/undertakes'; کارزار is the object 'battle' — together 'Siyavash wages battle for vengeance.' Note the classical use of کند as a present-narrative verb.
segments.s221-g182: کجا او بپروردش اندر کنار — کجا 'where' introduces a (rhetorical/relative) question linked to the previous clause; او is 'he'; بپروردش is the verb پروردن with the prefix بـ (classical present/subjunctive marker) plus the object clitic -ش 'him' and here functions as 'reared/raised him' or 'brought him up'; اندر کنار is a locative phrase 'at (his) side / beside' (اندر = 'in/within', کنار = 'side/beside'). The verb form is an archaic presentive used in narrative to mean 'raised/kept at his side.'
segments.s221-g183: ز مردان کنند آزمایش بسی — ز (poetic form of از) 'from/of' or 'among' introduces the group being acted on; مردان 'men' (here general men/heroes) is the object group; کنند is the plural present/habitual 'they do/make' (classical narrative); آزمایش 'a test/ordeal' is the object; بسی here conveys a large amount (poetic 'much/many'). The clause literally: 'From men they make many tests' = 'They subject many men to trials.'
segments.s221-g184: سلیح ورا برنتابد کسی — سلیح 'weapon/arm'; ورا is a pronominal/adverbial 'toward him/against him' (indicating the target of the arms); برنتابد is an archaic negative-able form meaning 'cannot endure/withstand' (here used impersonally); کسی 'no one/anyone' functions as the indefinite subject in negative sense. The overall sense: 'No one can withstand his weapons.' Note the poetical inversion and archaic verb formation (بر- + نتابد).
segments.s221-g57: This is a short nominal comparative clause: 'X به از Y' = 'X is better than Y.' Word order puts the item being praised (پیاده, 'infantry/man on foot') first, then the comparative marker به followed by از and the thing compared (چون تو پانصد سوار, 'five hundred horsemen like you'). The phrase چون تو is a simile/qualification 'such as you.'
segments.s221-g58: بدین روز و این گردش کارزار is a temporal/adverbial phrase 'on this day and in this turn (or round) of battle.' بدین is the older form (ب + این) meaning 'in/at this,' گردش کارزار = 'turn/circuit of battle' — together they locate the comparison in time and context.
segments.s221-g59: کشانی بدو گفت is a reporting clause: the (proper) name or nickname کشانی acts as the speaker, بدو = 'to him/unto him,' and گفت here functions as the past reporting verb 'said.' با تو سلیح = 'with you (as regards) weapon/arms' (i.e. 'as for weapons on you').
segments.s221-g60: نبینم همی جز فسوس و مزیح is direct speech reported by the previous segment. نبینم is first‑person present (negative) 'I do not see'; همی is an emphatic/old adverb (roughly 'indeed/at all'); جز = 'except/other than'; فسوس و مزیح are the only things he claims to see — 'idle whispering/talk and mockery.'
segments.s221-g61: Another reporting clause: بدو گفت رستم = 'Rostam said to him.' The conjunction که introduces the content of what Rostam says; تیر و کمان lists the objects ('arrow and bow') that he tells the addressee to consider.
segments.s221-g62: ببین is an imperative 'look/see.' تا هم‌اکنون = 'until/up to this very moment' (temporal phrase). سرآری زمان is an archaic verbal nominal construction: سرآری (old form) = 'you come/are placed at the head' and زمان = 'time' — together: 'see whether, up to now, time has set you at the head/put you forward.'
segments.s221-g63: چو = 'when.' نازش = ناز + ش = 'his beloved/pride' (a poetic way to refer to a cherished thing, here his horse), به اسپ گرانمایه = 'to/at the precious steed' (گرانمایه modifies اسپ). دید is the simple past 'he saw.' The whole clause gives the temporal trigger for the following action.
segments.s221-g64: کمان را به زه کرد is a light‑verb construction meaning 'he made (the bow) to the string' = 'he strung the bow' (را marks کمان as definite object). و اندر کشید is sequential: 'and (then) he pulled/drew it in' — اندر is an older prepositional/adverbial particle 'in/within' used with the verb کشید (here 'drew'). Both verbs are past tense narrative.
segments.s221-g65: The clause begins with the indefinite subject 'یکی' (here 'someone/a certain one'), followed by the past verb 'تیر زد' ('shot an arrow'). The phrase 'بر بر اسپ اوی' is a prepositional phrase marking the target: two occurrences of the preposition 'بر' are used poetically to mark direction/attack 'at/on', 'اسپ' is the noun 'horse' and the enclitic 'اوی' is a possessive pronoun 'his', so together 'بر بر اسپ اوی' = 'at his horse.'
segments.s221-g66: 'که' introduces a relative clause describing that horse. 'اسپ اندر آمد' uses the verb 'آمد' with the prefixed locative/adverb 'اندر' ('came in/advanced'); the sequence 'ز بالا' ('from above') uses the poetic preposition 'ز' and 'بالا' 'above', and 'به روی' ('onto/upon') marks direction toward a surface/face. Altogether the clause reads 'which the horse came upon from above.'
segments.s221-g67: This clause places the verb before the subject in classical poetic word order: 'بخندید' (he laughed) followed by the subject 'رستم'. The adverbial phrase 'به آواز' ('in a voice / aloud') modifies the next verb, and 'گفت' is the past-tense verb 'said' introducing direct speech.
segments.s221-g68: The particle 'که' introduces the quoted command. 'بنشین' is an imperative ('sit down' / 'be seated'). 'به پیش' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before' or 'in front of / in the presence of', with 'به' marking direction and 'پیش' the place. 'گرانمایه جفت' is a noun phrase 'the precious/esteemed pair' (adjective before the noun in poetry), so the whole line is the imperative 'Sit before the noble pair.'
segments.s221-g69: 'سزدگر' is a conditional/optative particle meaning 'if it be fitting' or 'if you deem fit'. 'بداری' here is a subjunctive/jussive form of 'داشتن' (second person singular) meaning 'you keep/you would keep'. 'سرش' is 'his head' with the enclitic pronoun 'ش'. 'در کنار' is the prepositional phrase 'at the side of / beside.' The clause thus gives a conditional recommendation: 'If you think it proper to keep his head at his side...'.
segments.s221-g70: 'زمانی' is an adverbial 'for a while'. 'برآسایی' is a noun meaning 'relief' or 'respite'. The preposition 'از' introduces the source 'from', and 'کارزار' is 'the battlefield' or 'battle'. Together the phrase means 'for a while a respite from the battle', the predicted result or benefit in the conditional construction.
segments.s221-g71: 'کمان را' is the definite object marked by 'را'. The verbal phrase 'به زه کرد' is a classical periphrastic expression meaning 'he made [it] to the bowstring', i.e. 'he strung the bow', with 'کرد' as the past tense auxiliary. 'زود' is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the action. 'اشکبوس' is an epithet/nickname (literally 'tear-kisser') placed after the verb phrase in apposition or as a descriptive tag; poetic lines often place epithets post-verbally.
segments.s221-g72: 'تنی' here is an indefinite/partitive 'a certain body' (i.e. 'a body'), followed by the intensified descriptive 'لرز لرزان' ('trembling, trembling' — repetition for emphasis). The conjunction 'و' links this phrase to 'رخ سندروس': 'رخ' is 'face' and 'سندروس' is a fragrant-wood metaphor/adjective (sandal/scented wood), so the clause paints the trembling body and the sandalwood-like face as further description of the figure.
segments.s221-g33: خروشید is a 3rd-person past-tense verb ('he/it roared' or 'he cried out') used to introduce spoken words. کای is a vocative ('O') addressing the addressee; مرد is the noun 'man' used here in the heroic sense ('warrior/valiant man'); رزم آزمای is a postpositive adjectival phrase ('battle-testing' / 'tried in battle'), so the whole phrase addresses someone as 'O battle-tested man.'
segments.s221-g34: هم is an adverb 'also/too' modifying the following verb; آوردت is classical past of آوردن with the enclitic -ت (2nd-person object) meaning 'he brought you' or 'you were brought' (object clitic is attached directly); آمد is the past tense 'he came' and here links the narrative clause to the injunction that follows; مشو is a negative imperative 'do not become / do not go' and together with باز ('back/again') + جای ('place') yields the command 'do not go back to your place' (i.e. do not retreat).
segments.s221-g35: کشانی is derived from کشاندن ('to draw/bring') and functions here as a verbal/agentive reference to the person who had drawn/led (literally 'he who drew'); بخندید is 3rd-person past 'he laughed'; و is the conjunction 'and'; خیره is an adjective 'staring/astonished'; بماند (classical form with ب-) is 3rd-person past/preterite 'he remained' — together 'he laughed and stood staring/astonished.'
segments.s221-g36: عنان را (object marked by را) 'the reins'; گران کرد is literally 'made (them) heavy' — a poetic way of saying he pulled/tightened the reins; و = 'and'; او را بخواند uses object-marking را with او ('him') and بخواند (classical past) meaning 'he called' or 'he summoned' — 'and he called to him.'
segments.s221-g37: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (meaning 'to him/unto him'); گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical past); خندان is an adverbial/participial descriptor 'smiling'; که introduces the direct question; نام تو چیست؟ is the standard formula 'what is your name?' with چی 'what' + ست (copula 'is').
segments.s221-g38: تن 'body' modified by بی‌سرت 'headless' (بی‌سر = without head); را is the definite object marker; که ('who') introduces the rhetorical question; خواهد + گریست is a periphrastic future/auxiliary construction (خواهد = 'will') with گریست (to weep/lamen t) — together: 'Who will (in the future) weep for your headless body?'
segments.s221-g39: تهمتن is the hero's name/epithet (here the speaker) and is the subject; چنین 'thus/so' modifies the manner of response; داد is the past tense 'gave' (here used with پاسخ); پاسخ 'answer' — the phrase together: 'Tahmatan thus gave an answer' (subject–verb–object order).
segments.s221-g40: چه 'what' begins the question; پرسی is 2nd-person present (classical form) 'you ask' — 'What are you asking?'; کزین پس = 'from this on / henceforth'; نبینی is negative present/subjunctive form of دیدن with the negative prefix نب- meaning 'you will not see' (classical usage often expresses future sense); تو is the pronoun 'you' (emphatic here after the verb); کام 'desire/fulfilment' is the object — 'From now on you will not see fulfilment (of your desire).'
segments.s221-g25: This clause describes a concomitant circumstance. به + noun phrase functions adverbially ('with' / 'holding'); همی is a premodern/historical particle marking continuous or habitual action ('still', 'keeps on'); کند is the simple present/habitual verb 'does/makes' placed at the end as usual. The phrase as a whole = 'with (a) wine-cup in hand he continually does sword-play.'
segments.s221-g26: میان + noun puts the following noun in the sense 'among/between'; یلان is the plural noun 'heroes' (object/scene), سرفرازی is an abstract noun 'glory/honour', and کند is again the habitual/present verb 'does' that ties the predicate back to the preceding clause. Word order places the preposition before the noun and the verb at the end.
segments.s221-g27: This is an interrogative clause. چرا 'why' starts the question, شد is the past/simple verbal 'became' used here as a perfective of شدن, کنون 'now' is temporal, and the predicate 'روی چون سندروس' (face like sandalwood) places the simile with چون before the comparison noun. The order is interrogation particle → verb → time → subject/comparison phrase.
segments.s221-g28: This is a short comparative question. سواری 'riding/horsemanship' is the topic, بود is the past copula 'was', and کمتر از is the comparative 'less than' with از introducing the standard of comparison (اشکبوس). The sentence asks whether one thing is inferior to the named standard.
segments.s221-g29: Imperative/occasional vocative construction: تو (you) is topicalized, followed by قلب سپه را where را marks the definite object 'the heart/core of the army'. به‌آیین is an adverbial phrase 'in due form / properly', and بدار is an imperative meaning 'keep/hold/maintain' (archaic/useful as a command). The word order places the addressee first and the verb at the end.
segments.s221-g30: This clause is first-person and states an immediate intention. من (I) + اکنون (now) gives the temporal frame; پیاده here is an adverb/adjective 'on foot, dismounted'; کنم is the 1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن used to express immediate future or intention ('I will do / I will cause'); کارزار 'battle/fight' is the object. The verb ending concludes the clause.
segments.s221-g31: This clause uses an older past form فگند (archaic variant of فکند) meaning 'he threw/hurled'. The phrase کمان به زه را groups 'bow with its string' (به used adverbially/'with') and را marks it as the object; به بازو indicates the target/location 'onto his arm'. Word order places the object before the verb, as is common in Persian.
segments.s221-g32: This short clause describes the following action: به بند کمر 'at/to the band of the waist' (بند کمر = belt/waistband) uses به as a locative/target marker, بر is the preposition 'upon/against', and بزد is the simple past 'he struck/smote'. تیر چند is a quantifying noun phrase 'several arrows' (چند = some/several) that is the object of بزد.
segments.s221-g121: برادرم is the possessive form 'my brother' (برادر + -م). هومان is the proper name in apposition; Persian allows the possessor and name adjacent. بسی پیش here is a time/adverbial phrase 'long/long ago before' (بسی intensifies پیش), and طوس is the person (Ţūs) before whom the action took place; the word-order is Topic — Name — Adverbial — (preposition omitted because پیش takes a following noun).
segments.s221-g122: جهان کرد is a poetic verbal phrase: جهان (ʼthe worldʼ) + کرد ('made/did') used idiomatically as 'showed himself to the world / made a display'. بر here is the preposition 'on/upon', and گونه آبنوس is a noun phrase 'an (ebony) cheek' with گونه 'cheek' and آبنوس qualifying it. The whole clause states the manner/appearance with which he presented himself.
segments.s221-g123: به ایران functions as a topical prepositional phrase 'as for Iran / with respect to Iran'. ندانم is the 1st-person present 'I do not know' (negative of دانستن). که here introduces the content clause 'that/who', and این مرد کیست is the embedded question 'who is this man?'; ست is the classical copula 'is' (۳rd-person).
segments.s221-g124: بدین لشکر is an older/poetic contraction of به این لشکر 'to/within this host/army' (ب = به + دین = این combined in the manuscript segmentation). او را is the object 'him' with the definite object marker را. هم‌آورد here means 'equal / match' (used as predicate meaning 'to be a match'), so the clause asks 'who is his equal in this army?'. کی ست is the final interrogative 'who is it?'.
segments.s221-g125: شوم is an optative/subjunctive first‑person form ('may I be' / classical marker for 'I will' or 'let me'), used here as a modal auxiliary introducing the speaker's intention. بازپرسم in context is a verb 'I will ask again' (باز‑ + پرسیدن), though the lemma provided is a noun; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of', and پرده‌سرای is a compound noun 'parde‑sara' (the curtained chamber / inner apartments), the source of the information to be asked.
segments.s221-g126: بیارند is a 3rd-person plural present/jussive of آوردن 'they bring / may they bring' (here 'they may bring / return (a report)'). ناکام modifies the following noun meaning 'unsuccessful / defeated'. نامش is 'his name' (نام + enclitic -ش). به جای is a prepositional phrase 'in its place / back (to the place)'. Together the clause says they may return his name as 'unsuccessful' in the report.
segments.s221-g127: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. پر اندیشه is a postpositive adjectival phrase 'full of thought' (پر = full of, followed by a noun). و joins the phrase to روی زرد, where روی 'face' takes the adjective زرد 'pale/wan' — typical Persian places adjectives after the noun.
segments.s221-g128: بپرسید is a past-tense form 'he asked' (ب- prefix + پرسید). زان is a prepositional/adverbial 'from that / about that', introducing the object or topic of the question. نامداران مرد is a noun phrase 'the renowned/illustrious man' (نامداران qualifying مرد). The clause thus reports that he asked that famed man about the matter.
segments.s221-g89: بزد is the 3rd-person singular past of زدن 'to strike'. The following prepositional particle بر marks the target ('upon/against'); in this poetic line بر appears doubled as part of the rhythmic/metrical phrasing and then heads the phrase بر سینهٔ اشکبوس 'upon the breast of Ashkabus'. و simply links the elements; سینه اشکبوس is the noun phrase 'the chest of Ashkabus' (proper name serving as possessor).
segments.s221-g90: سپهر is the subject 'the sky/heavens'; آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then'. دست او is an object phrase 'his hand' and داد is the past tense 'gave'; بوس is the noun 'a kiss' that is the thing given. Word order here is somewhat topical (time and subject fronted) but the construction is simply 'the heavens, at that moment, gave his hand a kiss.'
segments.s221-g91: This is a sequence of personified nouns speaking. قضا and قدر are personified forces ('Fate'/'Destiny') that take the speech verb گفت 'said'. گیر is here used as an utterance (imperative 'seize/take') after گفت. و links the two clauses. The line therefore reports short spoken commands attributed to abstract powers.
segments.s221-g92: فلک (the heavens) and مه (the moon) are again personified subjects with the speech verb گفت 'said'. احسنت is an interjection/exclamation 'well done' attributed to the heavens; زه is an interjection (here a short exclamation) attributed to the moon. The coordination و connects the two reported exclamations.
segments.s221-g93: کشانی is a proper name (here 'Koshani' or similar) and هم 'also' accompanies it. اندر زمان is the temporal phrase 'at that time'. جان بداد is a light-verb construction where جان 'life' is the object and بداد (past form of دادن) means 'gave' — idiomatically 'gave his life' (i.e. died or sacrificed his life).
segments.s221-g94: چنان شد is a fixed phrase 'it came to be so / it happened thus'. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / as' and گفتی here is the 2nd-person past 'you said' used to recall an earlier remark ('as you said'). ز مادر نزاد is a short clause 'born of a mother' (ز = 'from/of', مادر = 'mother', نزاد = past/participial form related to 'be born'), so the whole line reads 'it happened as you said: not born of a mother' (here نزاد carries the birth sense).
segments.s221-g95: نظاره is a noun 'the sight/that which is seen'; بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase 'upon/against them' or 'before them'. دو رویه سپاه is a nominal phrase 'the two-faced / two-fronted host' (دو modifies رویه, 'two-faced' or 'two-fronted', followed by سپاه 'army'). Word order is poetic; the noun phrase describes what the looking/scene is.
segments.s221-g96: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. دارند is the present 3rd-person plural 'they have' (possess/have). پیکار گردان is a compound where پیکار 'battle' is modified by گردان 'turning/rousing' (participial/adjectival), and نگاه 'gaze' is the head noun: 'who have a battle-turning gaze' — i.e. their look can turn the course of battle.
segments.s221-g193: همی is a habitual/progressive particle here (continually/keeps); the phrase نام ببر is a juxtaposition where ببر functions as the name/word being spoken (here 'Babr'/'tiger') and نام is 'name'. بیان is 'statement/utterance' and خواندش is a perfective verb with enclitic object -ش, 'he read/recited it to him.' Word order follows Persian SOV elements but the progressive همی comes fronted for poetic effect.
segments.s221-g194: ز here means 'from/by' (poetic preposition). خفتان is a noun/verbal noun form related to 'sleep' (lit. 'his sleeping'), joined by و 'and' to جوشن 'armor'. فزون qualifies جوشن as 'abundant/plentiful'. داندش is third-person singular present 'he knows/recognizes it/him' with enclitic -ش. The clause gives the means by which he is recognized.
segments.s221-g195: The paired negatives نه ... نه mean 'neither ... nor'. سوزد is third-person singular present (or general present) 'it burns' and در آتش is the locative 'in fire'. نه از آب‌تر uses از + آب‌تر (comparative 'wetter from water') to say it does not get wetter when exposed to water.
segments.s221-g196: چون here means 'when/as'. بپوشد is the present/subjunctive form 'he puts on/wears' (the b- prefix marks the present). شود is auxiliary-like 'it becomes' (third-person). برآیدش is a compound verb (برآمدن) in third-person with enclitic -ش 'it/for him rises/appears', and پر is the noun 'feather'—so the clause says that upon wearing it, a feather (or feathers) rise on him.
segments.s221-g197: یکی is an indefinite determiner 'a certain' or 'one (a)'. رخش is the name/word for a warhorse (Rakhsh) and دارد is present 'has/possesses'. به ... اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'beneath/under, inside' (به + زیر + اندرون), placing the horse under/underneath in the inner space; word order is noun + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s221-g198: تو is the 2nd-person 'you'. گفتی here functions as a past-tense verb 'you said' (in this lexicon list it appears as 'conversation/dialogue' but context is the verb 'said'); روان شد is a light-verb construction meaning 'set out / departed' (روان 'moving/away' + شد 'became/did'), and که introduces the clause content 'that Bisotun' (بیستون = Bisotun). The whole segment reports what 'you said' about his departure to Bisotun.
segments.s221-g199: همی again marks habitual/continuous action 'constantly/continually'. آتش is 'fire' and افروزد (افروختن) is a lexical present 'he kindles' (third-person). از خاک و سنگ is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'from/out of earth and stone'—the verb takes what is kindled from.
segments.s221-g200: نیارامد is a negative past/present-stative compound meaning 'was not pacified / would not be calmed' (نی- prefix + آمدن literal 'did not come (to calm)'). از بانگ 'by/through the cry' gives the cause/instrument, and هنگام جنگ is a time phrase 'at the time of battle'. Together: he was unpacified by cries in battle.
segments.s221-g137: چو introduces a conditional/temporal clause ('if/when'); رستم نباشد uses the negative subjunctive/old-fashioned negative of بودن to mean 'Rostam is not (present)'. مرا باک نیست is a typical Persian predicative sequence where the experiencer 'مرا' (to me) precedes the noun 'باک' ('fear') and the copula 'نیست' negates it: literally 'to me fear is not'.
segments.s221-g138: ز introduces an agent/source ('from/by'); گرگین و بیژن are proper names joined by و. دل‌م is the possessed noun 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م) and چاک نیست is a predicative phrase: 'is not torn/gashed' with چاک as the predicate noun/adjective and نیست the negative copula.
segments.s221-g139: سپه را topicalizes 'the host/army' (را here marking topic/emphasis). دو رزم is a quantified noun phrase 'two combats/battles', and گرانست is an archaic predicative adjective meaning 'is heavy/weighty/important' placed after the noun; پیش works adverbially here ('before/in front of' or 'in the presence of').
segments.s221-g140: بجویند is a third-person plural form of جستن used generically (classical Persian often uses plural with هر کس for general statements): 'each/people seek' or 'let each seek'. هر کس is the indefinite/generic 'every/each person'. بدین is the fused prepositional/ demonstrative (ب + دین) meaning 'by/with this' and نام خویش is a possessive phrase 'the name of himself' (خوی + ش = 'his own'), so the whole means 'each seeks this name for himself'.
segments.s221-g141: وزان is a fused conjunctional/locative phrase ('and/from that'); جایگه is a noun 'place/spot'. پیش + proper name (کاموس) means 'before/in the presence of Kamus'. رفت is simple past third-person singular 'he went'. The sequence gives a motion away from one place toward the named person.
segments.s221-g142: به نزدیک is a locative prepositional phrase 'to/near' (literally 'to the vicinity of'); what follows (منشور و فرطوس تفت) functions as the location — here likely proper names or obscure/place terms joined by و. In classical/poetic Persian such obscure names may appear without further inflection; the phrase as a whole specifies the destination/nearby place.
segments.s221-g143: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so'. گفت here functions as the reportative 'said/it was said' (classical usage), and کامروز can read as a temporal adverb 'that day/today' or a proper name depending on context; رزمی بزرگ is a noun phrase 'a great martial contest' (رزمی 'martial' modifying the noun, بزرگ 'great' as qualifier).
segments.s221-g144: برفت here is an older/poetic perfective-like verb meaning 'took place/occurred' (classical usage); و links clauses. پدید آمد is a two-word verbal compound 'appeared/came forth' (پدید 'appear' + آمد 'came'). از introduces the source ('from'), میش is 'ewe', and گرگ is 'wolf' — together 'a wolf came forth from an ewe.'
